courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: History of England: From Nation to Empire
courses.description:
This course provides a framework of the history of England for nearly two thousand years. It is hoped that through this course students will be able to broaden their knowledge of some key aspects of the history of England integrated into SDGs themes.

Students, thus, will be more connected to the modern world by understanding the past since some important ideas and inventions are legacies from England. Students will discuss from different perspectives of 'England' in this course, such as political development, intellectual ideology, cultural influences and transformation of society in modern Britain. For example, the concept of 'democracy' is based on the constitution movement and it still affects the modern world (#16 Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions)The industrial revolution and the changing the modern consumption. (#8 Decent Work and Economic Growth #9 Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure). Women's suffrage in the United Kingdom was a pioneer women movements (#5 Gender Equality)It is hard to understand the shaping of modern world without knowing the role of England in the WWII.Finally, the discussion of the role of Britain in colonial Hong Kong also provides an opportunity for students to understand the legacy of Britain in Hong Kong today.

Students should be able to review the following SDGs themes, including #5 Gender Equality #8 Decent Work and Economic Growth #9 Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure #16 Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions after this course, which fulfills the SDGs goals.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
Not for History Majors.
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.course_attributes:
SDG-GE #5 Gender Equality
SDG-GE #8 Decent Work and Economic Growth
SDG-GE #9 Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure
SDG-GE #16 Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Introduction: What is England?
2. Who are the English? Myths and the coming of Anglo-Saxon
3. The formation of the State: The Magna Carta and the Origin of Democracy **SDG #16 Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions**
4. England and her neighbours: Scotland, Wales and Ireland
5. Black Death in England: The emergence of consumption culture **SDG #8 Decent Work and Economic Growth**
6. Split with Europe? The Reformation and the Church
7. The emergence of Empire: The encounter of the New World
8. The development of London: A mega city in Europe
9. What did the Victorians leave us? How the Industrial Revolution and technology changed the world #9 Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure
10. What did the British leave us? Colonialism in Hong Kong
11. War, State and Society 1900-1945
12. Post war pop culture: From Britten to The Beatles **SDG#5 Gender Equality**
13. Conclusion: Contemporary Britain and her role in the world

courses.required_readings:
Lecture 1 and 2: Nelson, J. L., 'Anglo-Saxon England c. 500-1066' in *The Oxford Illustrated History of Medieval England,* ed Saul, Nigel (Oxford, 1997), pp. 26-60.

Lecture 3: Danziger, D. & Gillinger, J., 'Introduction' in *1215: The Year of Magna Carta* (London, 2003), pp. 1-13.

Lecture 4: Mandler, P., 'What Makes a Nation? Race and Civilization' in *The English National Character* (New Heaven, 2006), pp. 72-85.

Lecture 5: Goldberg, J., 'The Black Death and its Aftermath 1348-1481' in *Medieval England:* A *Social History* 1250-1550 (London, 2004), pp. 161-173.

Lecture 6: Newcombe, D. G., 'Why a Reformation?' in *Henry VIII and the English Reformation* (London, 1995), pp. 7-21.

Lecture 7: Queen Elizabeth 'Speech to the Troops at Tilbury' in *Elizabeth I: Collected Works* ed. L. S. Marcus and J. Mueller (2000, Chicago), pp. 235-236.

Lecture 8: Pepys, S., *A Shorter Pepys: Extracts from the Diary* (London, 1929), pp. 217-222.

Lecture 9: Mitchell, S., 'Technology, Science, and the Urban World' in *Daily Life in Victorian England* (Conn, 1996), pp. 71-86.

Lecture 10: Tsang, S., 'Government and Politics in Hong Kong: A Colonial Paradox' in *Hong Kong's Transitions,* 1842-1997 ed., J. M. Brown and R. Foot (London, 1997), pp. 62-83.

Lecture 11: Robbins, K., 'The Last Great British War' in *The Eclipse of a Great Power Modern Britain* *1870-1992* (London, 1994), pp. 182-190.

Lecture 12: The Beatles 1 (CD, EMI, 2002)

Lecture 13: Cannadine, D., 'Diana, Princess of Wales ' in *History in Our Time* (New Heaven, 1998. pp. 76-88.

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Understanding Guangdong-Hong Kong-Macao Greater Bay Area

meetings.instructor:
Professor LEE Wai Ying Joanna, 
Professor YEUNG Wai Chung Henry

courses.description:
(UGEC1120 is double-coded with GRMD1301.)

Since the establishment of the Special Administrative Region in 1997 under “One Country Two Systems”, the synergy between Hong Kong, Macao and the PRD has been renewed. With the recent Central Government's initiative of the development of Guangdong-Hong Kong-Macao Greater Bay Area, there will be closer and mutually beneficial co-operation among cities in this economic region. This will reshape the socio-economic relations and the urban landscapes of the three places. Studying the changing relations of Hong Kong, Macao and the PRD will not only help students understand the dynamic socio-economic development of the Greater Bay Area as a rapidly growing mega-city region in the world, but also capture the latest transformation of the region. A wide variety of teaching and learning activities, including lectures, forums and video watching will be conducted to present a lively and comprehensive picture of the Greater Bay Area to students.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGEC1120 is double-coded with GRMD1301. 
Not for Geography and Resource Management Majors; and students who have taken GRMD1301[1033].
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.course_syllabus:
Lecture topics:

Getting to know Hong Kong and the Pearl River Delta

• Hong Kong, Macao and the PRD: The geographical setting and changing spatial relationship (香港、澳門與珠江三角洲:地理位置與空間關係轉變)

• From farming to manufacturing: socio-economic transformation of the Guangdong-Hong Kong-Macao Greater Bay Area (從桑基魚塘到世界工廠：粵港澳大灣區經濟社會變遷)

Economic aspect

• Foreign direct investment: angel or devil? (外來投資：天使或魔鬼？)

• CEPA and Greater Bay Area (CEPA 與粵港澳大灣區)

• Guangdong-Hong Kong-Macao Greater Bay Area: a premier tourist destination (粵港澳大灣區:旅遊業新景象)

Social and environmental aspects

• Cross-border infrastructure development in the Guangdong-Hong Kong-Macao Greater Bay Area: competition vs cooperation (粵港澳大灣區跨境基建：全面規劃還是各施各法？)

• Migration in the Guangdong-Hong Kong-Macao Greater Bay Area (粵港澳大灣區人口流動)

• From state to market: how to satisfy the housing demand in the Guangdong-Hong Kong-Macao Greater Bay Area (從國家分配到市場機制：如何滿足粵港澳大灣區人口的住房需求?)

• Environmental degradation – a necessary evil of economic growth? (環境惡化–經濟增長之必然代價？)

Governance and planning

• Planning sustainable cities and region in the Guangdong-Hong Kong-Macao Greater Bay Area (粵港澳大灣區城市與區域可持續發展規劃)

• One country, many systems: competition or cooperation in the Guangdong-Hong Kong-Macao Greater Bay Area ‘一囯多制’：競爭還是合作？

• Mega-city regions: the Guangdong-Hong Kong-Macao Greater Bay Area from global perspective (全球視野中的粵港澳大灣區城市群發展)

Two video watching and discussion sessions

• 商機無限

• 走進大灣區。機

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Thinking Gender: An Introduction to Gender Studies
courses.description:
(UGEC1209 is double-coded with GDRS1001.)

This course aims to develop students’ ability to analyze the everyday life through the lens of gender. Drawing on the interdisciplinary nature of Women’s and Gender Studies, the course will guide students to examine how gender works as one of the primary social organizing principles in the movies we watch, the housework we do, the behaviors we express and many other daily activities that have been routinized. The first three lectures will equip students with a critical gender lens by questioning biology and getting familiar with theories of gender role, socialization, gender schema, doing gender and queer theory. All concepts to be explored in class will be explained with reference to research. At the end of the course, students will come to understand how political their personal life is. In particular, the course hopes to inspire students to take part in creating social change for gender equality, which is in line with the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGEC1209 is double-coded with GDRS1001.
Not for Gender Studies Majors; and students who have taken ANTH2310, GDRS1001, GENA2192 or UGEC2653.
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Japanese Culture and Society
courses.description:
(UGEC1232 is double-coded with JASP1100.)

The main objective of this course is to give students a general idea of Japanese cultural history and social background of modern Japan. Guidance leading to a better understanding of Japan will be provided.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGEC1232 is double-coded with JASP1100. 
Not for Japanese Studies Majors; and students who have taken JASP1100, SOCI2103 or UGEC2260. 
Co-requisite: UGFH1000, UGFN1000 or UGFN1001 (only applicable to students admitted to the 4-year curriculum)
courses.learning_outcomes:
1) Students are expected to gain an understanding of the main areas of Japanese life including work, family, gender roles, marriage, and religion. They are expected to understand the ways in which Japanese see their own society in terms of economic growth, Japan's place in the world, social order, and political structure.

2) Students are expected to understand and be able to use a variety of social theories and perspectives in understanding Japan. They are also expected to have gained an understanding of how these same perspectives might be used to analyze their own society, or other societies which they may wish to study in the future.

3) Students are expected to develop a clearer understanding of their own values regarding work, leisure, marriage, family, love, and religion through a comparison with the debates and competing interpretations of these values in Japan.

Remarks:

Department Board members disagreed with the suggestion that they replace the word 'understanding' with another word such as 'identity, explain, analyze or synthesize' as suggested by UGE.
courses.course_syllabus:
* *Week 1: Images of** **Japan**

This lesson introduces students to widespread popular images of Japan in Asian and Western media and academia. It considers how and why these images have changed over time and asks students to consider the usefulness and limits of generalizations about other people and societies.

* *Week 2: Nation, Nationalism and the Imperial System**

This lesson introduces students to theories explaining nationalism. It then uses these theories to consider nationalism in Japan. It explores the Japanese Imperial system in the context of historical developments in Japanese nationalism.

* Read:*1)Akemi Nakamura. "Textbook Fight Not as Simple as it Seems" *The* *Japan* *Times.*7 August 2005.

2)Reiji Yoshida. "Panel Set to Ponder Female on the Throne." *The* *Japan* *Times.*25 January 2005.

* *Week 3: The "Economic Miracle"**

This lesson explores two main theories behind Japan's economic development and subsequent recession of the 1990s. It considers the reasons for Japan's economic success, the experience of this success for Japanese people, and asks students to consider competing interpretations of Japan's economic development.

* Read:* Kenneth B. Pyle, "Japan's Postwar National Purpose," chapter 3 in his *The-Japanese Question: Power and Purpose in a New Era*pp.20-41

* *Week 4: Law and Order**

This lesson examines changing perceptions of social order in Japan. It explores the catalysts behind these changing perceptions and considers the relationship between perceptions and reality. Students are asked to consider how Japan and their own societies maintain trust in the government, faith in social justice, and belief in the reliability of public order.

* Read:*1 )Wolfgang Herbert. "Yakuza and the Law" In Harumi Befu, Tom Gill, Jeffery Eades (eds.). *Globalization and Social Change in Contemporary* *Japan**.* Pp. 143-158.

2)Michael Williams. "Revolving Door: Many Japanese Banks Ran Amok While Led by Former Regulars. *Wall Street Journal (Eastern edition).* Jan 19, 1996.

* *Week 5: Work and Workplaces**

This lesson introduces competing interpretations of Japan's employment strategies. It urges students to consider the reasons behind these competing interpretations. It also helps students to consider the system from the perspective of Japanese workers according to gender, age, and educational level, and encourages them to consider their own strategies and values as future members of their society's workforce.

* Read:*1)Joy Hendry. "Careers and Continuity" in *Understanding Japanese Society* pp. 149-167

2)Yamada, A. "Death of a Salaryman" *Japan* *Quarterly.* Jul-Sep 1999 vol. 46 (3): 40-47

3)Mathews, Gordon. "Seeking a Career, Finding a Job: How Young People Enter and Resist the Japanese World of Work" In Gordon Mathews and Bruce White (eds.) *Japan* k *Changing Generations: Are Young People Creating A New Society?*

4)Yuko Ogasawara. "Gossip" in *Office Ladies and Salaried Men.*pp. 70 - 97.

* *Week 6: Education**

This lesson introduces five theories that explain the role of education in society. Students are asked to consider how these theories may be useful in understanding Japan's educational system and philosophies. Students are encouraged to consider their own beliefs and principles regarding the purpose of education and their strategies **as** students-within theu educational system.

* Read:*1)Joy Hendry. "The Education System" in *Understanding Japanese Socieiy pp.*96-114

2)Lee Cunin, Maria. 2004. "The Students Academic Experience: Students' Thoughts on Reform and the Future." In her *Student Views in* *Japan**: A Study of Japanese Students* ' *Perceptions of their First Years at University**.*** Pp. 119-138

* *Week 7: Home and Family**

This lesson introduces several theories regarding the family in modem society. It then explores changes in Japanese family forms over time. Students are asked to articulate their own values toward love, marriage, and family in contrast to what they are learning about Japan.

* Read:* 1) Joy Hendry. "The House and Family System" in *Understanding Japanese Society* pp.22-41

2) Anne Allison. "Family and Home" chapter 6 in *Nightwork: Sexualify, Pleasure and Corporate Masculinity in a* *Tokyo* *Hostess Club* pp. 102-113

* *Week 8: Women and Gender**

This lesson examines changes in women's and men's roles and ideals in Japanese society over time. Students are urged to reflect upon their own understandings of gender in comparison to perceptions and practices of gender common in Japan.

* Read:*1)Surniko Iwao. "Myths and Realities " in *The Japanese Woman: Traditional Image and Changing Reality.*pp. 1-30

2)Wiseman, Paul. USA *Today**.*** 'Wo Sex Please, We're Japanese." 2 June 2004.

* *Week 9: Leisure and Play**

This lesson explores the role of leisure in modem societies and the development of the concept and practices of leisure in modem Japan. Leisure in Japan is explored in terms of gender, age, class, and generational differences. Students are encouraged to articulate their own attitudes toward leisure in relation to work, to consider how perceptions of how leisure have changed over time in their society, and to discuss how they expect their own leisure practices will change asthey age.

* Read:* Anne Allison. "A Type of Routine." In *Nightwork.* P. *42-56.*

* *Week 10: Mass Culture and Popular Culture**

This lesson explores the development of mass culture in Japan and explains the ways in which popular culture may serve as the basis for both social change and the reproduction of the status quo. Students are asked to consider how their own participation in popular culture and mass culture both generates changes in their societies and reproduces global capitalist structures.

* Read:* Merry White. "The Marketing of Adolescence in Japan: Buying and Dreaming." In Lise Skov and Brian Moeran (eds.) *Women, Media and Consumption in* *Japan**. P.*255-273

* *Week 11: Religion**

This lesson explores basic religious traditions and philosophies in Japan and how the role of religion in Japan has changed over time. It considers the role of religion at the level of state politics as well as in everyday life. Theories regarding religion in modern society are introduced. Students are urged to develop their own interpretations of the role of religion in modern societies and in their own lives.

* Read:*1)Joy Hendry. "Religious Influences," *Understanding Japanese Society.* pp. 115-132

2)Pfeiffer, William Sanborn. "Makihari: New Religion and Japanese Popular Culture" *Journal of Popular Culture.* Fall 2000 vol. 34. Iss. 2, pp. 155 – 174

* *Week 12: Student Presentations**

* *Week 13: Conclusion**

This lesson reviews the social issues which Japanese consider to be most serious such as the emergence of diversity, subcultures, and changing youth expectations and desires regarding adulthood. It discusses, through surveys, how Japanese people view their society and how they view their future. It asks students to consider which problems are seen as the most critical in their societies, how their own societies view their future, and how popular views about their societies and about their societies' future are related to the beliefs held by individuals.

* Read:* (Read one-quarter of letters) "Rethinking the War Experience." In Frank Gibney. (ed.) *In SensG: The Japanese Remember the Pacific War Letters to the Editor of the Asahi Shimbun.* pp. 283-318.

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Japanese Popular Culture in Cultural Globalization
courses.description:
(UGEC1271 is double-coded with JASP1160.)

This course examines the development and academic significance of various forms of Japanese popular culture such as films, comics, music, TV dramas and culinary culture, from multidisciplinary perspectives, including history, cultural anthropology, cultural studies and media studies. It aims to deepen understanding the following three facets of Japanese popular culture:

1) the applicaton of cultural globalization theory in Japan; 2) the global strategy of Japanese cultural industries; and
3) social and intellectual transformation of contemporary Japanese society.

Using concrete examples and grounding on representative academic writings, this course discusses the globalization of Japanese popular culture and relevant issues in indigenization, glocalization, hybridization, transnational cultural flows and media-mix. Students are trained to understand the nature and cultural implications of Japanese popular culture in an Asian context. Besides lectures, discussions and readings, this course selectively utilizes topical essays (projects), e-learning, audio-visual materials and fieldwork-based assignments as supplementary teaching tools.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGEC1271 is double-coded with JASP1160. 
Not for Japanese Studies Majors; and students who have taken JASP1110 or 1160.
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.learning_outcomes:
Through a historical and cultural analysis of different facets of Japanese popular culture, this course aims to achieve the following three academic goals:

1)It aims to use Japanese popular culture as a medium or stepping stone to enhance students’ interest in and understanding of Japanese history, society and culture.

2)It aims to deepen understanding of nature and impact of Japanese cultural industry and its cultural implications in an Asian context.

3)It aims to discuss Japanese popular culture within the theoretical framework of cultural globalization. Having taken this course, students will be equipped with a basic ability to analyse cultural phenomenon in the lens of such cultural concepts as indigenization, glocalization, hybridization, transnational cultural flows, media mix, cultural colonialism and orientalism.
courses.course_syllabus:
* *Main Objective****:**

This main purpose of course is to examine the global popularization and localization of Japanese popular culture from historical, cultural and comparative perspectives within the theoretical framework of cultural globalization. It aims to indicate that the cultural flows between Japan and Asia are by no means one-way traffic and Asia has been interacting and collaborating with the Japanese in creating, manufacturing, marketing and distributing cultural products for decades. Lectures, discussions and readings highlight different facets of cultural acculturation and transnational interaction found in Japan-Asia cross-cultural flows, such as appropriation, hybridization, global localization business strategy, the making of localized editions, the local application of Japanese formula, joint production, and the participation of foreign artists and staff. Solidly grounding on empirical data as well as theories from cultural globalization and cross-cultural studies, this course will be a pioneering teaching and learning project to explore uncharted research territories in Japanese studies, Japan-Hong Kong relations, and cultural studies.

* *Key Issues and Problems****:**

The following key issues and problems regarding the globalization of Japanese popular culture in an Asian context will be addressed in this course within the theoretical fiarnework of cultural globalization:

1) The idea that the influx of Japanese cultural products is a form of cultural imperialism that aims to expand Japanese economic interest and to spread Japanese value and lifestyle in Asia will be empirically and theoretically reexamined and challenged. It shows that cultural imperialism is an outdated model in cross-cultural studies, because it fails to understand the dynamic mechanism in transnational cultural flows and cultural forces of localization and hybridization.

2) While using "glocalization" (Roland Robertson uses it to refer to the localization strategy employed by multi-national companies to promote their products in different nations) to investigate the global business strategy of Japanese companies such as Sony, Nintendo, Capcom and Bandai in Asian and global markets, this course underscores the limitation of the glocalization theory in the East Asian context, as it fails to see the initiative taken by Asian artists and businessmen to creatively and selectively domesticate Japanese elements.

3) This course uses Japanese popular culture as a mirror or text to decode social and cultural issues of contemporary Japan. For examples, themes about ethnicity, gender, social hierarchy, political ideology, and cultural identity will be examined in the lens of Japanese popular culture.

* *Core Content, Structure and Selected Topics****:**

The course content of this course investigates the three facets of Japanese popular culture: (1) the application of cultural globalization theory in Japan, (2) the global strategy of Japanese cultural industries, (3) social and intellectual transformation of contemporary Japanese society. Accordingly, its structure follows the design of core content and contains the following three parts:

Part 1: Japanese Popular Culture and Cultural Globalization: Theories and Practices

Selected Topics:

(a) Introduction to Basic Theories in Cultural Studies and Cultural Globalization

(b) Globalization of Japanese Popular Culture in an Asian Context

(c) Reexamining the Cultural Imperialism, Orientalism and *Nihonjinron*Discourses

(d) Indigenization and Hybridization of Japanese Popular Culture in Asia (1) Food and Fashion

(e) Indigenization and Hybridization of Japanese Popular Culture in Asia (2) TV Programs

Part 2: Interplay between Globalization and Japaneseness

Selected Topics:

(a) Nihonjinron in Japanese Movies and Alternative Mode of Globalization

(b) The Formation of Japanese Manga and Anime and Their Hybridity

(c) The Making of an Otaku Culture in Japan and Beyond

(d) The Adaptation of Chinese Tradition in Japanese popular culture

Part 3: The Global Strategy of Japanese Cultural Industries

Selected Topics:

(a) Glocalization of Japanese Video Games and Cyber Culture

(b) Transnational Collaboration and Cross-cultural Themes in Japanese Movies

(c) Japaneseness and Universality in Japanese Comics and Animated Series

(d) Exporting and Domesticating Japanese Formula in Asian Drama and Music

(e) Media-mix asa Business Strategy in Japanese Cultural Industries

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Understanding Japanese Languages and Culture
courses.description:
(UGEC1281 is double-coded with JASP1120.)

This course explores the relationship between Japanese language and culture. Everyday communication strategies adopted by people in Japanese society will be examined in terms of Japanese language expressions, styles and structures. Linguistic approaches will be the principal analytical tools employed in this course but interdisciplinary approaches will also be adopted. This course is designed for students who are interested in knowing more about Japan but who have very limited or no knowledge of the Japanese language. Student will gain an understanding of the basic structure of the Japanese language, learn how usage of the language reflects Japanese culture and behavior, and explore how communication patterns of Japanese speakers differ from speakers of other languages.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGEC1281 is double-coded with JASP1120. 
Not for Japanese Studies Majors; and students who have taken JASP1120. 
Co-requisite: UGFH1000, UGFN1000 or UGFN1001 (only applicable to students admitted to the 4-year curriculum)
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Students are expected to have gained a basic understanding of the structure of the Japanese language and historical changes of the writing system. Moreover, students will be able to understand the usage of the language, different styles and when each should be used, and different strategies used in expressing politeness.

2. Students are expected to understand the relationship between the language expressions and the behaviors of Japanese people in various circumstances and the reasons behind such behaviors.

3. Through comparing the culture of Japan with that of Hong Kong, students will become able to make comments on the characteristics of each and distinguish between the two.

4. Students will be able to perform an elementary study regarding Japanese culture and this will help the students to develop his/her further study in Japanese Studies.
courses.course_syllabus:
* *Week 1**

* *Lesson 1**

Introduction to Japanese culture:

This lesson explains how Japanese culture influences daily life in Hong Kong today. The usage and the characteristics of Japanese language will also be discussed.

* *Week 2**

* *Lesson 2**

Introduction to the historical changes of kanji and kana in the Japanese language:

This lesson explains the historical changes of kanji and kana in Japanese language. Focus will be on why Japanese absorbed Chinese characters into their writing system and the process of how Chinese characters were converted to the modern *kana (hiragana and katakana)*.

* *Week 3 – Week 4**

* *Lesson 3**

Introduction to the phrases expressing linguistic politeness:

This lesson briefly explains the phrases expressing linguistic politeness. These include honorific expressions, expressions used in making requests, making apologies, expressing gratitude and personal opinions, giving advice and asking for permission. Emphasis will be placed on when and how these expressions are used. The relationship between such expressions and the characteristics of hierarchy in Japanese society will also be discussed.

* *Week 4 - 5**

* *Lesson 4**

Strategies used in making requests and refusals in Japanese:

This lesson discusses how Japanese make requests and refuse the requests of others. Such expressions are often used in daily life by Japanese but are not easily understood by foreigners. Special attention will be given to:

a. the difference in behaviour between Japanese and non Japanese in making requests and refusals.

b. how this behaviour reflects characteristics of Japanese culture.

* *Week 6 – Week 7**

* *Lesson 5**

Strategies used in making apologies and taking responsibility:

This lesson discusses how Japanese apologize and take responsibility after misconduct such as scandals or accidents. For example, we often see the top management of Japanese companies bow to the public to apologize for their misconduct and they may even quit the company in order to take responsibility for problems. In Hong Kong, it is rare to see such a phenomenon. We will discuss the mechanism of how Japanese make an apology and the effect of the Japanese way of apologizing upon social situations. Special attention will be given to the comparison of the styles of apology in Japanese and Hong Kong societies. We will also discuss the relationship between the Japanese way of apologizing and issues related to the Sino-Japanese War.

* *Week 8 – Week 9**

* *Lesson 6**

Ambiguity in the Javanese language:

Compared with other languages such as Chinese, there are many ambiguous linguistic practices in Japanese. These include, for example, the deletion of the subject "*watashi*" (I), the use of "*koto ni*

* narimashita*" (become something) rather than "*koto ni shimashita*" (I decided to do something) when talking about personal matters such as getting married, and the frequent use of intransitive verbs rather than transitive verbs. Most Japanese language learners including Hong Kong learners have experienced difficulties with these expressions and practices in their learning process. We will analyze and comment on this phenomenon.

* *Week 9 – Week 10**

* *Lesson 7**

The Business Manner of Japanese:

This lesson discusses how Japanese behave in business negotiations. Japanese are often criticized for not stating their true feelings and opinions. They often use expressions such as "*kangaesasetekudasai*" (Please let me think about it), "*kekkoo desu*" (It's O.K. /No, thank you) to indirectly express their opinions. We will discuss why Japanese behave in such a way and the correct way to deal with a Japanese business partner.

* *Week 10 - Week 11**

* *Lesson 8**

Japanese language in a sociolinguistic perspective:

In this lesson we discuss the following items of importance in sociolinguistics as they appear in the Japanese language.

a. the comparison between written and colloquial styles

b. the comparison of language usage for men and women

c. the comparison between the standard language and dialect

d. the comparison of language usage between the young and the elderly

e. the comparison between formal styles and informal styles

f. the comparison between jargon and standard expressions

* *Week 12**

* *Lesson 9**

The success and failure in learning Javanese language (with a focus on Hong Kong Cantonese speaking learners):

First, we will give a brief introduction to the theories of second language acquisition. Next, the factors affecting the results of the process of second language learning will be discussed. To ensure a better understanding, case studies comparing English language acquisition among Japanese and Hong Kong people will be introduced. Finally, by summarizing the above observations and the characteristics of both Japanese and Cantonese, we will comment on the difficulties experienced by Hong Kong Cantonese speaking Japanese learners.

* *Week 13**

* *Lesson 10**

Special topics on Japanese 1anguage and Japanese culture:

Special topics will be selected to help students understand the relationship between Japanese language and culture.

a. *"shin go ryuukoogo taishoo"* (New words and buzz terms Grand Prix)

b. *"kotoshi no kanji"* (The Chinese character of this year)

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Japanese Cinema

meetings.location: William M W Mong Eng Bldg 803
William M W Mong Eng Bldg 803

courses.description:
(UGEC1302 is double-coded with JASP1140.)

Film is regarded as an important medium for understanding culture and society. This course examines how representative works of Japanese cinema communicate fundamental aspects of Japanese culture and society as well as the human condition. Students will learn how this communication is made possible through the art of cinematic form (i.e., mise-en-scene, cinematography, editing, and sound), the common language shared among filmmakers around the world. The course introduces the basics of film analysis, giving students critical-thinking skills that will enable them both to see how Japanese cinema engages with world cinema and to understand how Japan—especially after the post-war—becomes a hub for myriad cultural flows.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGEC1302 is double-coded with JASP1140.
Not for Japanese Studies Majors; and students who have taken GESC2080, JASP1140 or 1150.
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.learning_outcomes:
| 1. **Identify** representative works of Japanese cinema. 2. **Evaluate** fundamental aspects about Japanese culture and society as represented in Japanese film. 3. **Consider** the relationships between Japanese film and world cinema through analysis of film language and cinematic technique. 4. **Develop** critical thinking and writing skills through discussion, classwork, essay writing, and a final project. |
| --- |
courses.course_syllabus:
Course format: Films will be made available for students to view outside of class as homework. Short film clips will be shown during class time for discussion and analysis. Each lesson begins with an introduction of the filmmaker and the film’s context in Japanese film history. Each lesson considers the manifold ways Japanese film engages with world cinema, in terms of form (i.e., mise-en-scene, cinematography, editing, sound) and/or content (e.g., theme, character, genre, etc.). In this way, students will learn how Japanese films can engage audiences in ways independent of their cultural and linguistic specificities.

* *Week 1**

* *Topic: Introduction to Japanese Cinema and Film Analysis**

- What is Japanese cinema? How do we read a film? What is film as an art form? How does film represent fundamental aspects of culture and society (such as literature, art, men, women, the environment, and food)?

* *Unit 1 | Literature on the Big Screen**

* In Japanese culture, cinema has been inspired—and continues to be inspired—by works of literature from Japan and around the world. This unit explores the deep influence of literature on Japanese cinema. Topics may include literary aesthetics (i.e., mono no aware), tropes from drama (i.e., Noh, Kabuki) and painting (i.e., ukiyo-e), poetic structure (i.e., renga), and adaptation of narrative (i.e., novels).*

* *Week 2**

* *Topic: Noh Theater and Atmospheric Grief**

Discussion and analysis

- Kore-eda Hirokazu, *Maborosi* (1995)

Reading:

- Donald Richie, “Kore-eda’s *Maborosi*: Showing Only What is Necessary”

* *Week 3**

* *Topic: Ukiyo-e and the Pathos of Things (*Mono no Aware*)**

Discussion and analysis:

- Mizoguchi Kenji, *Sansho the Bailiff* (1954)

Reading:

- Dudley Andrew, “Mizo Dayū”

- Mark Le Fanu, “*Sansho the Bailiff*: The Lessons of *Sansho*”

* *Unit 2 | Japanese Women**

* Women play an important role in Japanese culture and society. This unit explores representations of women in Japanese cinema—from the figural (i.e., monsters, supernatural beings) to the realistic (i.e., demimonde, housewife, mothers, sisters, office lady)—and how such representations raise questions about gender roles and the relationship between self and society.*

* *Week 4**

* *Topic: Woman and Landscape**

Discussion and analysis:

- Teshigahara Hiroshi, *Woman in the Dunes* (1964)

Reading:

- Audie Bock, “*Woman in the Dunes*: Shifting Sands”

- Linda Ehrlich and Antonio Santos, “The Taunt of the Gods: Reflections on *Woman in the Dunes*”

* *Week 5**

* *Topic: Working-Class Women**

Discussion and analysis:

Naruse Mikio, *When a Woman Ascends the Stairs* (1960)

Reading:

- Moeko Fujii, “Step by Step: Hideko Takamine in *When a Woman Ascends the Stairs*”

- Phillip Lopate, “*When a Woman Ascends the Stairs*: They Endure”

* *Unit 3 | Japanese Men**

* Men play an important role in Japanese culture and society. This unit explores representations of men in Japanese cinema—from the archetypal (i.e., ninja, samurai, yakuza) to the realistic (i.e., soldier, husband, father, salarymen)—and how such representations play within established film genres (i.e., Hollywood Western, film noir, and melodrama).*

* *Week 6**

* *Topic: Ronin as Rogue Cowboy**

Discussion and analysis:

Kurosawa, *Yojimbo* (1961)

Reading:

- Donald Richie, “Yojimbo”

- Alexander Sesonske, “*Yojimbo*: West Meets East”

* *Week 7**

* *Topic: Gangsters and Film Noir**

Discussion and analysis:

Shinoda Masashiro, *Pale Flower* (1964)

- Discussion and film analysis

Reading:

- Chuck Stephens, “*Pale Flower*: Loser Takes All”

* *Week 8**

* *Topic: Salarymen and Melodrama**

Discussion and analysis:

Ozu Yasujirō, *An Autumn Afternoon* (1962)

Reading:

- Geoff Andrew, “*An Autumn Afternoon*: A Fond Farewell”

* *Unit 4 | Japanese Environment**

* The environment plays an important role in Japanese culture and society. This unit explores representations of the environment in Japanese cinema—from figural and fantastic responses to nuclear disaster (i.e., Godzilla) in the postwar era, to documentary-style meditations on ecological crisis in the present day.*

* *Week 9**

* *Topic: Radioactive Monsters**

Discussion and analysis:

Honda Ishirō, *Godzilla* (1954)

Reading:

- J. Hoberman, “*Godzilla:* Poetry After the A-Bomb”

* *Week 10**

* *Topic: Water and Myths of Nature**

Discussion and analysis:

Hamaguchi Ryūsuke, *Evil Does Not Exist* (2023)

* *Unit 5 | Japanese Food**

* Food plays an important role in Japanese culture and society. This unit explores the representation of food in Japanese cinema, investigating how food mediates human connection within Japan and around the world.*

* *Week 11**

* *Topic: Ramen and Community**

Discussion and analysis:

Itami Jūzō, *Tampopo* (1984)

Reading:

- Willy Blackmore, “*Tampopo*: Ramen for the People”

- Linda Erhlich, “Community and Connection: Itami Jūzō’s *Tampopo* (1985)”

* *Week 12**

* *Topic: Japanese Home-Cooking Across the Globe**

Discussion and analysis:

Ogigami Naoko, *Kamome Diner* (2006)

courses.required_readings:
Readings below will be made available in PDF form on Blackboard. See syllabus for unit descriptions. Each assigned reading is a short essay or chapter from a larger book. Students are required to read each essay in its entirety. Each lesson will reference parts of the reading to facilitate discussion of the assigned film.

* *Unit 1 | Literature on the Big Screen**

- Donald Richie, “Kore-eda’s *Maborosi*: Showing Only What is Necessary,” in

* Film Criticism* 3 (Winter/Spring, 2011): pp. 37-45.

- Dudley Andrew, “Mizo Dayū,” in Dudley Andrew and Carole Cavanaugh, *Sanshō Dayū* (BFI Publishing, 2000)

- Mark Le Fanu, “*Sansho the Bailiff*: The Lessons of *Sansho*” (Criterion)

* *Unit 2 | Japanese Women**

- Audie Bock, “*Woman in the Dunes*: Shifting Sands” (Criterion)

- Linda Ehrlich and Antonio Santos, “The Taunt of the Gods: Reflections on *Woman in the Dunes*,” in Dennis Washburn and Carole Cavanaugh, eds., *Word and image in Japanese Cinema* (Cambridge University Press, 2000), pp. 89-107.

- Moeko Fujii, “Step by Step: Hideko Takamine in *When a Woman Ascends the Stairs*” (Criterion)

- Phillip Lopate, “*When a Woman Ascends the Stairs*: They Endure” (Criterion)

* *Unit 3 | Japanese Men**

- Donald Richie, “Yojimbo,” in Donald Richie, *The Films of Akira Kurosawa* (University of California Press, 1999), pp. 147-155.

- Alexander Sesonske, “*Yojimbo*: West Meets East” (Criterion)

- Chuck Stephens, “*Pale Flower*: Loser Takes All” (Criterion)

- Geoff Andrew, “*An Autumn Afternoon*: A Fond Farewell” (Criterion)

* *Unit 4 | Japanese Environment**

- J. Hoberman, “*Godzilla:* Poetry After the A-Bomb” (Criterion)

* *Unit 5 | Japanese Food**

- Willy Blackmore, “*Tampopo*: Ramen for the People” (Criterion)

- Linda Erhlich, “Community and Connection: Itami Jūzō’s *Tampopo* (1985),” in Alistair Phillips and Julian Stringer, eds., *Japanese Cinema: Texts and Contexts* (Routledge, 2007), pp. 263-272.

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Tourism and Sustainable Development
courses.description:
(UGEC1372 is double-coded with JASP1190.)
Since 2015, tourism has been listed by the United Nations as one of the means to achieve sustainable development goals. How does tourism contribute to the sustainable development of human society? What are the impacts of tourism upon the cities and communities? Using Japan as a principal example, this course aims at examining the possibilities and challenges of achieving sustainable development goals through tourism. The first part of this course introduces basic concepts of tourism and sustainable development goals, and discusses their relationship. The second part of the course compares Japan to China and other countries, and investigates the benefits and costs of tourism in relation to sustainable cities and communities, indigenous culture, and natural resources. Additional discussions further explore the potentials of sustainable tourism in Hong Kong. With assigned readings and coursework, students are expected to acquire knowledge on sustainable development goals, and critically evaluate tourism as a means to achieve these goals.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGEC1372 is double-coded with JASP1190.
Not for Japanese Studies Majors; and students who have taken JASP1190.
Co-requisite: Any course with "UGFH" or "UGFN" as prefix (only applicable to students admitted to the 4-year curriculum)
courses.course_attributes: SDG-GE #11 Sustainable Cities and Communities
courses.course_syllabus:
Part One – Overview

Week 01: Overview: Why Do People Travel? How Does Traveling Relate to Sustainable Development Goals?

* This lecture topic introduces basic theories in social studies of tourism, and the current discussions on tourism in relation to sustainable development goals.

Week 02: Introduction to Sustainable Development Goals

* This lecture topic further examines the UN Sustainable Development Goals, and requests students to discuss the possible challenges of achieving these goals through tourism.

Week 03: The Sustainable Destination

* There is no tourism without destinations. This lecture topic examines features that make a destination sustainable.

Week 04: Overtourism? Cases of Kyoto, Macau and Venice

* Overtourism is one of the keywords that address the problems incurred by tourism. Drawing cases from both Europe and Asia, this lecture topic examines these problems, and invites students to suggest solutions.

Part Two – Sustainable Development and Tourism in Japan and Beyond

Week 05: Overview of Tourism in Japan

* This lecture topic introduces the socioeconomic background in the historical development of tourism in Japan. Drawing Japan as an example, students are encouraged to reflect on the social and political agenda of tourism.

Week 06: Gentrification: The Impacts of Urban Tourism

* This lecture topic examines the problem of urban gentrification and its relation to tourism in Japan, China and beyond.

Week 07: Mega Events

* Drawing on the Olympic Games as one of the examples, this lecture topic scrutinizes the effects and impacts of hosting mega events to attract tourists in Japan and beyond.

Week 08: Cultural Heritage and Local Communities

* This lecture topic scrutinizes the impacts of tourism on local communities at world cultural heritage sites.

Week 09: Environment and Ecotourism

* The lecture topic examines ecotourism and its impact on the local and global environment.

Week 10: Tourism and Indigenous Culture

* This lecture topic investigates the role and impact of tourism on the sustainability of indigenous culture.

Week 11: Tourism and Rural Revitalization

* This lecture topic focuses on the possibilities and challenges of introducing tourism for sustainable development in depopulating regions in Japan and beyond.

Week 12: Tourism and Disasters

* This lecture topic discusses the phenomenon of transforming places associated with tragedy into tourist attractions, its cultural meanings, and social impacts upon local communities in Japan and beyond.

Week 13: Discussion: Sustainable Development and Tourism in Hong Kong and Japan

* During the concluding lecture, students are invited to exchange their views on sustainable development in Hong Kong, in comparison to Japan and other localities discussed over the semester.

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Perspectives in Economics
courses.description:
(UGEC1511 is double-coded with ECON1010.)

This is an introduction to the nature of the economic problem and how society and man deal with the economic problem. It will cover resource allocation and the concept of economic efficiency, product and factor markets, macroeconomic indicators and the nature of macroeconomic problems, and macroeconomic policy. Economic concepts and theories will be applied to various social and economic issues such as marriage, crime and government policies.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGEC1511 is double-coded with ECON1010
Not for majors in Fac of Bus Admin or IDA + X Double Maj Prog, ECON, GLEF, QFRM Majors; & students who have taken DOTE/DSME1030,1040, ECON1010 or 2011.
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix.
courses.learning_outcomes:
It is expected that students will:

- acquire problem solving skills to think like an economist.

- master a set of concepts that will help them think more coherently and consistently about a wide range of problems

- be able to use these skills in their everyday lives as consumers, savers, entrepreneurs, investors, workers, citizens and participants in a global economy.
courses.course_syllabus:
Perspectives in Economics will introduce the fundamental concepts of economic and issues in economic research, covering both micro and macro economics. The course attempts to illustrate the importance and beauty of economics via a non-technical approach, using only elementary mathematics and graphs for expositions. Topics will include concepts of rationality, opportunity cost analysis, the functioning of supply and demand, the role of government and fiscal policy, the banking system and monetary policy, pricing, and some topics pertaining to crime, marriage and international trade. Students in this course will learn how to apply the fundamental economic principles to almost every aspect of their life.

WeekTopic

1Rationality, What, When, How, Whom

2Demand and Supply Theory

3Demand and Supply Application: Marriage

4Demand and Supply Application: Crime

5Demand and Supply Application: Rent Control

6Measuring Economic Performance: National Income Accounting

7Measuring Economic Performance: Inflation and Unemployment

8Money Creation and Banking System

9Monetary and Fiscal Policy

10Trade and WTO

11Contribution by Nobel Laureates

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Fundamentals of Government
courses.description:
(UGEC1512 is double-coded with GPAD1020.)

Introduction to the nature of political inquiry, the basic concepts in political science and the main features of the major types of political systems. Some emphasis is placed on the institutional aspects of government and administration, with concrete examples.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGEC1512 is double-coded with GPAD1020. 
Not for Government and Public Administration Majors; and students who have taken GPAD1020.
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.learning_outcomes: Students are expected to acquire from this course general and fundamental knowledge about what is politics, how inevitable is it in our daily life, how power is organized and diffused around us, and how to think politically.
courses.course_syllabus:

Erection of Modern States in the Developing World

Interest Groups, Political Parties and Electoral Systems

Legislatures, Executives and Bureaucracy

Constitution and Judiciary

Civil Society and Civil Disobedience

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Fundamentals of Public Administration
courses.description:
(UGEC1522 is double-coded with GPAD1030.)

This course introduces contemporary study and practice of public administration, and the characteristics and roles of public bureaucracy in various types of administrative systems. Students will also learn to analyze some issues such as codes of conduct for civil servants, budgetary politics and redress of administrative grievances.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGEC1522 is double-coded with GPAD1030. 
Not for Government and Public Administration Majors; and students who have taken GPAD1030 or UGEC1732. 
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.learning_outcomes:
After taking this course, it is expected that students will have developed the following capabilities and skills:

* By the end of this introductory course students should be able to develop broad sweep knowledge of a range of approaches to the study of public administration and, to a certain degree, an in-depth knowledge of selected concepts and theories. These theories were selected because of their direct relevance for developing an understanding of modern government behaviour.
* Public administration is often considered in a managerial manner, as little more than control and straightforward analysis of efficiency and economy. This is not necessarily the case. People from different walks of life tend to have different expectations of government. This course is intended to foster a change in attitudes among students. After the course, students are expected to have the ability and tendency to think critically from multidisciplinary perspectives and to provide a critical appreciation of the strengths and weaknesses of any theoretical approach.
* Most important of all, by taking this course, students should have developed an understanding of the basic concepts and theories in public administration and should have gained the ability to apply them to the context of Hong Kong. Public administration, as an area of study, is very much a study based in American context. Therefore, it is important for students to reflect on the concepts and approaches covered in the course, and to consider how they can contribute to a better understanding of public administration in Hong Kong.
* The course is intended to reinforce communication skills on the part of students. Through the training in debates and discussions, students should have developed the ability to convey abstract ideas and complicated issues in a concrete and organized manner.

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Government and Politics of China
courses.description:
(UGEC1540 is double-coded with GPAD1070.)

This is an introductory course that surveys key issues in Chinese politics, including the historical background of the system, the roles of the party/state, the style of leadership, the forms of popular participation, and the distinctive features of Chinese politics. The focus of this course is political change.

Classic essays by Sun Yatsen, Lu Xun, Mao Zedong and others provide students with grounding in the key problems modern China has faced, as well as the solutions it has pioneered. From that foundation, we will move on to contemporary institutions and intellectual debates over inequality, human rights, and the future shape of political reform in China.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGEC1540 is double-coded with GPAD1070. 
Not for Government and Public Administration Majors; and students who have taken GPAD1070.
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Basic Banking and Finance
courses.description:
(UGEC1560 is double-coded with ECON1420.)

This course is an overview of the institutional and practical aspects of the banking and financial sectors of the economy, with emphasis on how the two sectors impact on the economic well being of workers, consumers, and investors. This course is designed for students with no previous knowledge or training in economics. Topics to be discussed include: interest rate determination, operation and management of the banking system, banking policy and supervision, interbank market and liquidity, money supply, exchange rate systems and linked exchange rate, investment returns, risk diversification and portfolio choice, stock market movement, initial public offering and derivatives, financial ethics, international debt crisis, and their relationships with consumer prices, trade, investment, unemployment and the level of economic activity.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGEC1560 is double-coded with ECON1420
Not for majors in Fac.of Bus. Admin., IDA + X Double Maj. Prog., ECON,GLEF,QFRM Majors and stds who have taken ECON1420, 3410, 3420, 3520 or GESC2410. 
Co-req.: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Understand the basic operation of the banking system and financial markets and the importance of ethics and integrity.
2. Apply and interpret the basics of asset price valuation and method to financial instruments such as equity, debt, and derivatives.
3. Apply relevant concepts, tools, and techniques to analyze some major financial and banking activities and issues and to understand their impacts of on people’s livelihood.
4. Improve ability to interpret financial data.

1. 了解銀行體系和金融市場的基本運作及誠信和道德操守之重要。
2. 運用和解釋股票、債券、衍生金融工具等證券資產價格評價和證券估值技巧。
3. 運用相關概念，工具和技巧來分析一些重大的金融及銀行活動和議題，及了解其對人民生活的影響。
4. 提高閱讀和理解財務數據資料的能力。

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Marriage, Family and Kinship
courses.description:
(UGEC1625 is double-coded with ANTH1310.)
This course invites students to explore the issue of gender cross-culturally through the framework of kinship. It introduces basic concepts and major issues in the areas where the study of gender and the study of kinship intersect through an anthropological lens. Issues to be explored include kinship structures and related gender roles in cross-cultural perspective, the changing relationship between marriage and love, and the various new ways of people “doing” kinship in contemporary societies and the emerging social problems resulted from these changes. Themes such as kinship and the new reproductive technologies, fictive kinship and human-pet bond will all be discussed.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGEC1625 is double-coded with ANTH1310.
Not for Anthropology Majors; and students who have taken ANTH1310. 
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.learning_outcomes:
• Be familiar with the basic concepts, theories and contemporary social issues relating to gender and kinship.

• To gain appreciation of the culturally diverse forms and understanding of gender, marriage, family and kinship.

• Be able to identify and summarize key points and arguments in scholarly anthropological writings.

• To understand the practice of kinship as a means to ensure survival and establish meaningful social relations.

• To think analytically about the complex linkages between individuals and society in understanding and performing various forms of intimate relationships.
courses.course_syllabus:
This course introduces basic concepts and major issues in the areas where the study of kinship and the study of gender intersect through an anthropological lens. Issues to be explored include kinship structures and related gender roles in cross-cultural perspective, the changing relationship between marriage and love, and the various new ways of people “doing” kinship in contemporary societies and the emerging social problems resulted from these changes. Themes such as kinship and the new reproductive technologies, fictive kinship and human-pet bond will all be discussed. Overall, the course encourages reflection on the origin and nature of human social relations by elucidating the complex linkages between individuals and society in understanding and performing various forms of kinship.

* *PART I: BASIC CONCEPTS OF FAMILY AND KINSHIP**

* 1. Introduction: Concepts, Abbreviations and Symbols*

Issues to be discussed: What is Kinship? WHY do we study kinship, family and marriage? Kinship, family and marriage in modernity.

* 2. Unilineal (Patrilines and Matrilineal) and Cognatic Descent*

Issues to be discussed: Why is virginity so valued in patrilineal and cognatic descent groups? Do women have more power in matrilineal societies?

* *PART II: Kinship, Marriage, and Family Related Issues**

* 3. Choice of Mate, Courtship and Romance before Marriage*

Issues to be discussed: What is romantic love and how has the notion changed over time? With whom one falls in love? What are the other factors that people consider when choosing a marital partner? How has the technological development changed how people love?

* 4. The “Big Day” and Romance after Marriage*

Issues to be discussed: What is Marriage? Why is it nearly universal? How many people can you marry? With whom? By whom? The changing wedding rites and their meanings; Globalization and International Marriages.

* 5. Kinship, Social Relations, and Economy*

Issues to be discussed: How do various practices of kinship function as means to ensure economic survival and enhancement?

* 6. Gender, Power and Family*

Issues to be discussed: What is family? Why is it important? What are the mainly types of family and how are they related to particular social structures? How do family members bond together? What are the expected gender roles of wives and husbands in modern societies across the world? Gender and power dynamics within family.

* 7. Childhood, Parenthood and Social Reproduction*

Issues to be discussed: What are the changing roles of and expectations for children? What are the roles and practices of parents in different societies in terms of childrearing and social reproduction? How are the experiences and meanings of childhood and parenthood influenced by globalization?

* 8. Marriage “Troubles”: Incest, Extra Marital Sex, Divorce and Remarriage*

Issues to be discussed: Why the incest taboo? Are we really facing marital “crises” today?

* 9. Adoption, Fictive Kinship and Network of Care*

Issues to be discussed: Why adoption? Why and how do we create fictive kinship? What are the potential challenges? Is fictive kinship fictive?

* 10. New Reproductive Technologies and Kinship*

Issues to be discussed: Reproduction and conjugal relationships; Infertility and NRTs; Do NRTs give more power and freedom to woman?

* 11. Homosexuality and Kinship*

Issues to be discussed: Consider homosexuality cross-culturally; Is homosexuality “abnormal”?

Homosexuality, social discriminations and various strategies to “cope”.

* 12. The Elderly, Caregiving and Haven in a Heartless World*

Issues to be discussed: What are the social implications of an aging society? What is the comparative power of children, young adults, and the elderly within different societies? New Challenges faced by modern families in aging societies.

* 13. Conclusion: Biology, Culture and Kinship*

Issues to be discussed: Course wrap-up; Underlining the complex linkages between individuals and society in understanding and performing various forms of intimate relationships.

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Understanding Archaeology
courses.description:
(UGEC1633 is double-coded with ANTH1710.)

This course aims to provide students with some basic understanding of archaeology. It will introduce the basic theory, method and practice of archaeology. It will focus on the nature and development of archaeology, data acquisition (archaeological surveys and excavations), data processing and analysis, synthesis and interpretation (reconstruction of the past environmental ecology, economic, social and cultural life) and important archaeological discoveries and studies around the world. The significance of archaeology to contemporary societies will also be discussed.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGEC1633 is double-coded with ANTH1710. 
Not for Anthropology Majors; and students who have taken ANTH1710. 
Co-requisite: UGFH1000, UGFN1000 or UGFN1001 (only applicable to students admitted to the 4-year curriculum)
courses.learning_outcomes:
After taking this course, students are expected to:

Have a basic understanding of what archaeology is about.

Know the basic archaeological research methods and field methods.

Obtain basic knowledge on the developments of humans and cultures, the dynamics between nature, human and cultures, and what can we learn from the past experiences.

Appreciate the diversity and achievements of human societies and cultures.

Know how archaeology is relevant to modern societies.

Cultivate the ability of inter-disciplinary thinking, critical thinking, and the ability to conduct holistic analysis.

Have broadened the academic and intellectual horizon, and acquired new information/knowledge.
courses.course_syllabus:
* *Major contents of this course are as follows**

What really is archaeology? Objectives of archaeology as a scientific discipline vs. 'archaeology' created by Hollywood movies.

How did archaeology occur? -A brief history of modern archaeology.

What are the tasks of archaeologists? -Basic principles and research approaches of archaeology

Some very significant archaeological discoveries in the world, which have shaped our knowledge today.

Academic dynamics of archaeology and sciences, and archaeology and social sciences.

The meaning of archaeological remains and research in contemporary societies: how archaeological works are relevant to us?

Archaeology in the 21st century: achievements, new objectives, challenges, and the use and abuse of archaeology in the world.

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Drugs and Culture
courses.description: Many drugs have mind-altering effects but some are defined as legal and some as illegal. The War on Drugs have been launched in many societies but none of them has successfully become a drug-free society. This course provides students an understanding of drug-related issues from a perspective of medical anthropology. Through close readings of ethnographies, viewing of videos, and fieldtrips, the class explores drug use, prevention, and treatment in both scientific and cultural perspectives and re-examines the linkages between legal and illegal behaviours, healthy and unhealthy bodily conditions, as well as science and culture.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
Not for Anthropology Majors. 
Co-requisite: UGFH1000, UGFN1000 or UGFN1001 (only applicable to students admitted to the 4-year curriculum)
courses.course_syllabus:
The major concepts in this course include drug addiction, harm reduction, and medical anthropology. The core content includes:

Module 1: What is Drug?

Chapter 1&2 Introduction to Drugs

The first two chapters discuss the physiological and psychological effects of mind-altering substances and their use from “traditional” societies to modern societies.

Module 2: Drug Effects

Chapter 3&4 Addictive Drugs: Alcohol and Heroin

These two chapters introduce two highly addictive drugs, namely alcohol and heroin. We discuss their cultural consumption and harmful effects and how institutional mechanisms in different societies regulate these drugs.

Chapter 5&6 Psychoactive Drugs: Marijuana and LSD

These two chapters discuss the controversy over drug legalization, particularly marijuana and LSD. We discuss the effects of these drugs and whether their medical and/or recreational use should be legalized.

Module 3: Drug Prevention and Treatment

Chapter 7&8 Drug Scene in Hong Kong and China

These two chapters discuss the changing drug use patterns in Hong Kong and China and examine how these changes are interweaved with an array of social factors, such as migration and economic development.

Chapter 9&10 The War on Drugs vs. Harm Reduction

These two chapters introduce the common two approaches to handling drug use issues in a society: zero tolerance and harm reduction. We discuss the effectiveness of these two approaches.

Chapter 11&12 Health Consequences of Drug Taking

These two chapters discuss the associated risks of drug taking, including HIV/AIDS and HCV contraction. We also discuss the effectiveness of the treatment of these drug-related diseases.

Various topics in relation to drugs and culture, such as cocaine use, club drug use, ketamine use, misuse of prescription drugs, and use of new pharmaceutical products, such as Prozac and Viagra, are the selected topics for this course.

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Sociology and Modern Society
courses.description:
(UGEC1823 is double-coded with SOCI1102.)

This course explores into the significance of sociological perspectives in understanding modern society. Attention will be given to the application of sociological concepts in the observation and analysis of social phenomena in modern society. The course is designed particularly to develop intellectual skills, as part of a student's general education, for a better understanding of the nature of modern society.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGEC1823 is double-coded with SOCI1102.
Not for Sociology Majors; and students who have taken SOCI1001 or 1102.
Co-requisite: UGFH1000, UGFN1000 or UGFN1001 (only applicable to students admitted to the 4-year curriculum)
courses.learning_outcomes:
- Students will gain exposure to a diverse set of sociological research, and understand how the relevant concepts and theories can be applied to modern societies.

- Students will develop an understanding of the constitutive relations between the economic, political, social, and cultural domains in society, thereby gaining a new perspective on their own lives and the world around them.

- Students will learn to develop their own analytical framework through active participation in discussion and completion of essay type test questions. These will enhance their general knowledge and skills in critical thinking.
courses.course_syllabus:
CORE CONTENT

1. Sociology as a social science discipline

- What is a social science explanation?

- Variety of sociological explanations

2. Rise of modernity

- With special reference to the insights of Durkheim, Weber, and Marx.

3. The Internet society and globalization

- Nature of postindustrial society and information society

- Virtual community v. traditional community

- The small world phenomenon: Insights from social network research

4. Consumer society

- Nature of advanced capitalism

- Economic sociology of market demand

5. Marriage and family

- The changing conceptions of marriage and consequences of the changes

- Theoretical debates among such theories as sociobiology, social conflict, rational choice

6. Socioeconomic inequality and status competition in modern society

- The root sources of inequality: classic and contemporary theories

- Rising inequality in the USA and East Asia

- Status competition

7. Selected topics

- For examples: Theories and evidence on modern schooling, gender and work, deviance and social control, social movement, democratization, health and health care, third world development.

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Culture of Hong Kong
courses.description:
(UGEC1835 is double-coded with ANTH1410.)

This course introduces students to characteristic aspects of Hong Kong culture. It will cover a wide range of cultural changes that have taken place since WWII. The course starts with an introduction to anthropological research in Hong Kong, and looks at topics including village culture and urbanization, food culture, marriage, family and gender, religion, and popular culture. Underlying most topics are questions of the modern versus the traditional, rural versus urban, cosmopolitan versus parochial, and how all these opposites interact to construct culture in Hong Kong. Hong Kong has been developed at a rapid pace since WWII. This course also discusses to what extent the development in Hong Kong meets the 17 Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), in particular in the area of gender equality, cultural inclusiveness, and socially responsible production and consumption.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGEC1835 is double-coded with ANTH1410. 
Not for Anthropology Majors; and students who have taken ANTH1410. 
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.course_attributes:
SDG-GE #5 Gender Equality
SDG-GE #12 Responsible Consumption and Production
SDG-GE #16 Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions
SDG-GE #17 Partnerships for the Goals
courses.learning_outcomes:
After taking this course, students will be able to:

1. explicate the development of Hong Kong culture after the Second World War, including changes in the urban and rural areas, and critically identify the different factors affecting such development.
2. analytically discuss aspects of culture that they had previously taken for granted, including daily activities such as entertainment, food culture, and religious and secular rituals.
3. analyze different local lifestyles and their interaction with the changing environment, as well as the role of tradition and globalization.
4. identify and explicate aspects of the unity and diversity of culture in Hong Kong, and the processes that mold and shape cultural identity.
5. formulate research design on topics concerning Hong Kong, engage in cultural data collection, and present and communicate research results.
courses.course_syllabus:
### **Core Topics**

1. Hong Kong Culture in Overview (SDG17)
2. Social development in historical perspective
3. Chinese traditions
4. Food and foodways (SDG12, 16)
5. Marriage and family
6. Gender relations (SDG5)
7. Religion and belief systems (SDG16)
8. Migration and identity

* *Additional Selected Topics**

1. Heritage (SDG16)
2. Housing issues
3. Social values and mass media
4. Ethnicities (SDG16)
5. Occupations and socioeconomic development

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Government of Hong Kong
courses.description:
(UGEC1885 is double-coded with GPAD1050.)

This is an introductory course in government and politics of Hong Kong. It examines the following major issues: political institutions and processes; the various theoretical approaches to understand Hong Kong politics; the Basic Law and the principle of “one country, two systems”; the performance of the SAR Government since the return of sovereignty ; political culture; political parties and elections; and public administration and public policy. Emphasis is placed on recent changes and development, and the prospects for democratization.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGEC1885 is double-coded with GPAD1050.
Not for Government and Public Administration Majors; and students who have taken GPAD1050.
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.learning_outcomes:
(a) grasp the basic operation, composition and function of the political institutions like Chief Executive, Executive Council, Legislature, District Council and electoral system in Hong Kong

(b) understand past political developments, have a basic understanding of the political change and evolution in Hong Kong such as decolonization and parties development , and the current political issues

(c) use their understanding of the political system and political history of Hong Kong, e.g. political parties and electoral system, to analyze the current affairs of Hong Kong and political issues of the day.
courses.course_syllabus:
The course wants the students to acquire the basic skills and knowledge after taking this course:

(1)a basic understanding of the major political institution in Hong Kong: their function, composition, operation and evolution in the past;

(2)a basic understanding of past political developments

(3)a basic understanding of current political issues and debates

(4)ability to use the above knowledge to understand and analyse the current issues around them, and day-to-day public affairs in Hong Kong society.

* *Course Schedule**

* *Part A Government Institutions of Hong Kong**

1. The Basic Law as Mini-Constitution

Legal Basis of Basic Law

Major Contents and Controversies in the Basic Law

Readings:

1. 《中華人民共和國香港特別行政區基本法》，第一、二、三、五、八章。

2. Yash Ghai, *Hong Kong’s New Constitutional Order: The Resumption of Chinese Sovereignty and the Basic Law* (Hong Kong: Hong Kong University Press, 1997), pp.56-72.

2. The Chief Executive and the Executive Branch

Nature of the “Executive-Dominant” (行政主導) system

The Chief Executive and the Executive Council

POAS (主要官員問責制) and its Effects

Recent proposals on Political Appointment System

Reading:

劉兆佳，“行政主導的政治體制”，劉兆佳編，【香港二十一世紀藍圖】(中文大學出版社)，二零零零年，頁三至三十六。

3. The Legislative Council: Power and Functions

Composition and Operational characteristics

Powers and Functions of the Legislative Council

Changes in power after 1997

Reading:

Kathleen Cheek-Milby, *A Legislature Comes of Age: Hong Kong’s Search for Influence and Identity* (Hong Kong: Oxford University Press, 1995), Chapter 6.

4. Local Administration, DC and UC/RC

District Boards/Councils: Evolution, Powers and Functions

Recent Proposed reforms on DCs

Urban Council: History, Functions and Abolition

Reading:

Norman Miners, *The Government and Politics of Hong Kong* (Hong Kong: Oxford University Press, 1998), (Fifth Edition), pp. 155-177.

Midterm Examination: February 14 (Tentative)@10:30am

* *Part B Political Development and Change, 1960s to 1997**

* *5.****The Early Colonial Regime and the 1966-67 riots**

Politics and Society before 1967

The 1966 and 1967 Riots

The aftermath of the Riots and Colonial Reforms

* *Reading****:**

張家偉，《香港六七暴動回顧》(香港：太平洋世紀，二零零零年)，第十二章。

* *6.****The Sino-British Negotiations**

Path to Negotiations

Interests of China and Britain

Course of the Negotiations and the Joint Declaration

* *Reading****:**

Ma Ngok, "The Sino-British Dispute over Hong Kong: A Game Theory Interpretation," *Asian Survey* 37, 8 (August 1997), pp.738-751.

* *7.****Democratization and Democracy Movement up to 1997**

Student movement and pressure group politics in the 1970s

British Initiative: Development of Representative Government

Democracy Movement in the 1980s

Patten Reform and the Sino-British Dispute

Second Stove and the Provisional Legco

* *Reading****:**

Kuan Hsin-chi, "Power Dependence and Democratic Transition: the Case of Hong Kong," *The China Quarterly* 128 (1991), pp.774-93.

* *8.****Development of Party Politics in Hong Kong**

History of Party Development in Hong Kong

Major differences in parties' positions

Factors affecting Party Development in Hong Kong

* *Reading****:**

Lau Siu-kai and Kuan Hsin-chi, "Hong Kong's Stunted Political Party System," *The China Quarterly* 172 (2002), pp. 101 0-1028.

* *9.****The Evolution of Electoral System and Elections in Hong Kong**

Major Electoral Systems in the World

Evolution of Elections and Electoral Systems in HK

The Functional Constituency System

The Election Committee System

* *Reading****:**

馬嶽與蔡子強，《強舉制度的政治效果：港式比例代表制的經濟》(香港：城市大學出版社，2003)，頁三章。

* *Part C Post-1997 Politics**

* *10.****Post-1997 Governance**

The General Strategy and General Picture

One Country Two Systems in Practice: How High is High Autonomy?

Rule of law and Civil Liberties

Trends of Change

* *Reading****:**

Wong Yiu-chung, "'One Country' and 'Two Systems': Where is the Line?" in Wong Yiu-chung ed., *"One Country, Two Systems" in Crisis: Hong Kong’s Transformation since the Handover* (Lanham: Lexington, 2004), pp.9-31.

* *11.****The SAR Governance Crisis of Tung Chee-hwa**

Different Causes of the Governance Crisis: Institutional Factors, Environmental factors, Problems with strategy, policy and style, etc..

* *Reading****:**

Lau Siu-kai, "Tung Chee-hwa's Governing Strategy: The Shortfall in Politics," in Lau Siu-kai ed., *The First Tung Chee-hwa Administration: The First Five Years of the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region* (Hong Kong: Chinese University Press, 2002), pp. 1-39.

* *12.****Democratic Development after 1997**

The Impasse of the Democracy movement after 1997

Article 23 and the July 1 Rally

Struggle over Universal Suffrage, the 2004 NPCSC verdict, Subsequent Debates

The Donald Tsang Era

The 2007 NPCSC Verdict and Prospects for Democratic Development in HK

* *Reading****:**

Ma Ngok, "Democratic Development in Hong Kong: A Decade of Lost Opportunities," in Joseph Cheng ed., *The Hong Kong Special Administrative Region in Its First Decade* (Hong Kong: City University Press, 2007), pp.49-74.
