courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Experiencing the City Through Walking
courses.description:
The course introduces students to interdisciplinary studies on urban walks, urban encounters, cultural responses to urban walking experiences and creative interventions reacting to the urban built environment.

Since the second half of the 19th century, ways of encountering the city at the street level and various means for capturing and making sense of urban encounters have continuously evolved, the course’s first aim is to introduce to students the history and shifting paradigms for understanding urban experiences in relation to walking.

Secondly, the Covid-19 pandemic has substantively revived global interests in urban walks, the second aim of the course is to examine the latest ideas concerning rediscovering the city through different modes of walks.

Thirdly, students will learn connecting theories to practices, they will learn to become active “users” of the city and adopt different forms to create new walking experiences and articulate urban experiences.

More specifically, the course will focus on how ‘walking in the city’ has become an activity in its own right, and the cultural and social potentials of ‘non-functional’ urban walks. Wandering in the city has its own evolving knowledge and practices, the academic discussion accumulated through the years will be introduced.

As such, through urban walks one can encounter the heterogeneity and complexity of cities including but not limited to its built environment, symbolic dimension, flows, natural elements and publicness. The heterogeneity and complexity of the city will be primarily addressed, especially through various schools of thought which have influenced how urban walks have been undertaken.

The Sustainable Development Goal 3 ‘Good-Health and Wellbeing’ & Goal 11 ‘Sustainable Cities and Communities’ will be covered by the course, as urban walks have been seen as key to achieving wellbeing in recent years and it is also a means for the community to enhance their bonding by sharing local knowledge through walks. Goal 4 ‘Quality Education’ is also covered as walking is increasingly seen as a medium for innovative education and a form of life-long learning outside of the classroom.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
Not for Urban Studies Majors.
Co-requisite: Any course with "UGFH" or "UGFN" as prefix (only applicable to students admitted to the 4-year curriculum)
courses.course_attributes:
SDG-GE #3 Good Health and Well-being
SDG-GE #4 Quality Education
SDG-GE #11 Sustainable Cities and Communities
Experiential Learning Course
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. To contrast and evaluate the evolving discourses and transforming practices of urban walks emerged in different eras since the 19th Century
2. To differentiate and analyse different ways of seeing and approaches of observations when one undertakes urban walks (connecting to SDG 11)
3. To formulate and arrange one’s own style of urban walk in order to obtain a particular type of urban experience (connecting to SDG 3)
4. To synthesize and produce forms of expressions that capture urban experiences and urban encounters obtained from consciously designed urban walks (connecting to SDG 4)
courses.course_syllabus:
* *Week 1**

* *Introduction: The renewed interests in urban walks**

* In the first week the overall problem will be introduced, namely how there has been a resurgence of interests in urban walks and its proliferating diversity. In the first week, the introduction aims at demonstrating the interdisciplinary nature of the issue and the many dimensions of urban walk as an issue.

* *Week 2**

* *The idea of walking and conceptualizing urban walks**

* Before discussing urban walk specifically, in week 2 the literature on walking as a human behavior will be outlined, some of the key and foundational texts will be discussed.
* The early attempts to conceptualize urban walks in the 19th century will also be introduced, in particular, the idea of the Flaneur and Charles Baudelaire’s contribution will be outlined. The discussion on Flaneur will be followed by interventions made by the Surrealist, Walter Benjamin and others in the first half of second century.

* *Week 3**

* *Innovating urban walks in the 20th Century**

* Postwar interventions such as the Situationist International and Guy Debord’s thoughts on creative urban walks will be introduced, together with other modern artists and architectural theorists’ initial detour into the realm of experimental walks. The discussion will be put in contrast with Jane Jacobs’ ideas with more focus on urban planning and community.

* *Week 4**

* *A Japanese school of walk: Modernology and ‘Street Observations Studies’**

* While week 3 and week 4 largely focus on Anglo-European ideas, the substantial contribution made by Japanese artists and urban theorists will be introduced. In this week, students will practice ‘street observation’ on campus, as the initial attempt to put theory into practice.

* *Week 5**

* *Walkscapes: Contemporary art and the aesthetical turn**

* More artistic attempts to reinvent the practice of urban walks will be introduced in this week, in the lecture the ideas from performance studies will also be discussed to support the debates on aesthetical dimension of urban walks.

* *Week 6**

* *Walkability, well-being and the body**

* Functional dimensions of urban walks and policy related concerns will be discussed in this lecture. The lecture will also briefly introduce ideas about corporeal geography and non-representational theory, as the theoretical discussion will bring up the non-cognitive aspects of walk.
(Addressing SDG goal 3)

* *Week 7**

* *Field trip 1: Walks about capturing urban experiences and experimental walks**

* The field trip will be about more ‘conventional’ approach to urban walk as an aesthetic practice, it will be about how heterogeneous urban experiences and observations could be gained.
(Addressing SDG goal 4)

* *Week 8**

* *Heterogeneous urban experiences: Sensory and visual**

* The lecture will focus on the types of sensory and visual data could be gained from urban walks, the literature analyzing and conceptualizing these data and experiences will be outlined.

* *Week 9**

* *Cataloguing and expressing urban experiences: The idea of forms**

* The lecture will focus on how various forms are employed to catalogue and reorganize the urban experiences gained from urban walks. The forms include moving images, still images, literary experimentation, and research projects.

* *Week 10**

* *Field trip 2: Walk design and the idea of journey design**

* The field trip will be a practical guidance discussing how various forms of walks, guided tours and journey are designed and created. It will be followed by a more conceptual discussion on the experience economy and experiential cultural consumption.
(Addressing SDG goal 11)

* *Week 11**

* *Walking in Hong Kong**

* The lecture will explore how the idea of urban walk has been picked up and discussed in the context of Hong Kong. The initial idea of Hong Kong as a ‘City of Walk’ will be discussed.
(Addressing SDG goal 11)

* *Week 12**

* *Workshop: Presenting a walk design project (1)**

* The workshop will be an interactive session for students to showcase their creative ‘walk-design’ projects, the lecturer will give substantial feedback and moderate dialogues in order to foster imagination and creativity for creating potential cultural projects concerning urban walk and urban experiences
(Addressing SDG goal 4)

* *Week 13**

* *Workshop: Presenting a walk design project (2) & Conclusion**

* The workshop will be an interactive session for students to showcase their creative ‘walk-design’ projects, the lecturer will give substantial feedback and moderate dialogues in order to foster imagination and creativity for creating potential cultural projects concerning urban walk and urban experiences
* The conclusion will point to recent discussion on the future role of urban walks and cities that empower the community
(Addressing SDG goal 4)

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Foundations in Public Health
courses.description:
(UGEC1920 is double-coded with PHPC1001.)

This course will introduce undergraduate students to the discipline of public health and its basic academic framework, concept and methodology. The objective of the course is to provide a broad intellectual perspective of public health and presents both local and global public health challenges that are facing our society in the 21st century. The various academic disciplines within the domains of public health that include environmental health sciences, health policy and management, social and behavioral sciences and basic science will be introduced and discussed. The impact of these public health problems in relation to our society and our everyday lives will be emphasized. An analytic public health problem solving framework will be used to enable students to appreciate and learn the problem solving methodology in evaluating and appraising various public health problems.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGEC1920 is double-coded with PHPC1001. 
Not for MBChB; Public Health Majors; and students who have taken MEDF1021, 1120, PHPC1001 or UGEC2930.
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.academic_org: JC Sch of Pub Health Prim Care
courses.learning_outcomes:
By the end of this course, the students should be able to:

1. Define public health, identify its major domains, functions, and disciplines, and explain its historical development and contemporary achievements.

2. Describe the concepts of transitions, and analyze the impact, challenges, and strategies for controlling infectious and non-communicable diseases.

3. Examine the impact of physical activity and sedentary behavior on fall risks in older adults and assess interventions promoting physical activity and health among school-aged children.

4. Describe the ethical principles in health and healthcare, including social and distributive justice

5. Analyze the social determinants of health and health equity, and evaluate population health and high-risk intervention strategies.

6. Describe the basic concepts of health demography and analyze the associations between population attributes and population health

7. Health System

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: History of Macau: The Encounter of Eastern and Western Cultures
courses.description: Macau is renowned for its gaming industry, yet it is also the place where East meets West. After more than three centuries of social evolution and cultural exchange, which attributes to Macau’s social diversity and valuable cultural heritage. This course will be divided into two parts. Part I traces and examines the development of Macau from a historical perspective, foster students an understanding of the contexts that shape the society and culture of the city from the past to the present, and how these contexts served as obstacles or opportunities in achieving the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). Part II exposes students to selected themes, including ethnic relations, economic activities, religious and cultural exchange, heritage conservation, etc., exploring Macau’s social diversity and cultural inclusiveness, and examine their relevance to the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), including SDG#8 Decent Work and Economic Growth, SDG#10 Reducing Inequality and SDG#11 Sustainable Cities and Communities.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
Not for History Majors.
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.course_attributes:
SDG-GE #8 Decent Work and Economic Growth
SDG-GE #10 Reducing Inequality
SDG-GE #11 Sustainable Cities and Communities
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Develop an understanding of the history of Macau and the evolution of its society.
2. Recognize and critically analyze how and why various contexts shaping the culture and society of Macau, and how these contexts served as obstacles or opportunities in reducing social inequality (SDG#10 Reducing Inequality) and promoting economic growth (SDG#8 Decent Work and Economic Growth).
3. Appreciate Macau’s cultural diversity and cultural heritage.
4. Evaluate and reflect on selected issues (e.g. economic development versus heritage conservation, challenges of preserving Macanese cultures in a Chinese city, etc.) concerning the sustainable development of Macau and its community (SDG#11 Sustainable Cities and Communities).
courses.course_syllabus:
(1) Introduction: Encounter of Eastern and Western Cultures

Part I: Development of Macau: From A Historical Perspective

(2) The Coming of the Portuguese

* Portugal in the Age of Discovery: Exploring the Route to the East
* Early Encounters of China and Portugal

(3) Macau in the 16th and 17th Centuries

* The Opening of Macau
* The Chinese and the Portuguese: Duality in the Governance of Macau

(4) Macau in the Late Qing Period

* Opium War and Its Consequences Towards Macau
* Coping with New Order: From Governor Amaral to the Sino-Portuguese Treaty of Peking

(5) Contemporary Macau

* Sino-Portuguese Relation in the 20th Century and the Handover of Macau
* Conflict and Cooperation over the Transition of Macau
* The Politics of Localization: Civil Service, Chinese as Official Language, and Law

Part II: The Social and Cultural Diversity of Macau

(6) The Chinese Society of Macau

* The Chinese Community in the Early Period
* The Rise of the Chinese Merchant Elite
* The Role and Contributions of the Chinese Merchant Elite in the Development of Modern Macau

(7) Macanese

* Who is “Macanese”? – The Origin and Evolution of the Macanese
* The Social Life and Culture of the Macanese
* Legacy and Sustainability of Macanese Communities and Their Culture in the 21st Century

(8) Missionary in Macau and Cultural Exchange

* The Jesuits in Macau: The Intermediary of Cultural Exchange between China and the West
* St. Paul College of Macau: The First Western University in the Far East
* Christ and Confucius: How did the Missionary Proclaim Christianity in China and How did the Chinese React?

(9) Folklore and Popular Religion in Macau

* *Mazu* and *Chu Tai Sin* Beliefs in Macau
* Connections between the Fisherman Customs and the Society of Macau
* Drunken Dragon Parade: Preservation and Inheritance of Intangible Cultural Heritage in Macau

(10) Macau and the Maritime Silk Road

* Emergence of the Maritime Silk Road and Global Trading Network
* The Importance of Macau in the Maritime Silk Road Trade
* Commodity Circulation and Interconnection between China and the World

(11) Gaming Industry in Macau

* Early Development of Macau’s Gaming Industry
* From Monopoly to Oligopoly: A Socio-economic Reconsideration of Gaming Liberation
* Prospects and Challenges: The Sustainable Development of Macau’s Gaming Industry after the Gaming Liberation

(12) Historic Centre of Macau and Heritage Conservation

* Development of the Policy and Legal Framework of Heritage Conservation in Macau
* Becoming “World Heritage”: The Historical and Cultural Significance of the Historic Centre of Macau
* The Impacts of UNESCO World Heritage Listing to the Sustainable Development of Macau

(13) Conclusion

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: The Effectiveness of the Law Intended to Safeguard Cultural Heritage in Hong Kong
courses.description:
Surprisingly, as one of the world’s most modern cities, often thought of as a city of skyscrapers, Hong Kong has an incredibly rich and diverse cultural heritage. This is reflected in its wide diversity of buildings and monuments; including ancient temples, walled villages, and legacies of its colonial period including Tai Kwun, Heritage 1881, and more recent glass and steel structures such as Norman Foster’s HSBC Building. Hong Kong also has many museums and galleries containing important representations of Chinese and other cultures which were important in the development of modern Hong Kong. These range from works of art, through to the seemingly more mundane legacy of Hong Kong’s colonial history, and evidence its pre and post-colonial heritage. This course considers the issues which face this heritage, and the legal framework and other protection affecting cultural heritage in Hong Kong. Among other topics, the course will consider: the law and policy intended to safeguard built heritage in Hong Kong; the law and policy affecting antiquities and other forms of portable heritage including private and public collections in Hong Kong; the law and policy affecting street art and public expressions of Chinese culture; the history of looted heritage including Hong Kong’s looted heritage and Hong Kong’s role in the illicit trafficking of looted heritage from China, and the law which is intended to prevent this illicit trade; the law and policy intended to safeguard Hong Kong’s water heritage including shipwrecks, and treasure hunting; and, the law and policy intended to safeguard the newest and richest recognised form of cultural heritage- intangible cultural heritage, including Cantonese opera, dragon-boat racing, bamboo scaffolding, neon signs and milk tea.
This course will consider the threats that Hong Kong’s cultural heritage faces and the law and other safeguarding measures that have been implemented and may be introduced to promote Sustainable Development Goals including, among others, Life on Land (SDG 15), Life Below water (SDG 14), Climate Action (SDG 13), and Sustainable Cities and Communities (SDG 11).
courses.enrollment_requirement:
Not for LLB students.
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.course_attributes:
SDG-GE #11 Sustainable Cities and Communities
SDG-GE #13 Climate Action
SDG-GE #14 Life Below Water
SDG-GE #15 Life On Land
courses.course_syllabus:
| 1 | **Introduction.** What is cultural heritage? What is culture heritage law? Cultural heritage in Hong Kong. Issues facing cultural heritage in Hong Kong. The development of international and domestic law to protect cultural heritage. Other measures intended to safeguard cultural heritage. |
| --- | --- |
| 2 | **Safeguarding Built Heritage in Hong Kong.** Problems with built heritage in Hong Kong in the colonial period. The development of law to safeguard built heritage in Hong Kong. |
| 3 | **Safeguarding Built heritage in Hong Kong since 1997.** 2006-7: the year of heritage social unrest. The legal framework of public and private measures safeguarding built heritage in Hong Kong today. |
| 4 | **Portable Heritage.** What is portable heritage? What laws affect the ownership, sale, gift and loss of cultural heritage in Hong Kong? Public and private ownership of portable heritage. The role of museums and archaeology in safeguarding portable heritage. |
| 5 | **Private ownership of portable heritage in Hong Kong.** What legal issues may arise in the buying and selling of Chinese antiquities in Hong Kong? |
| 6 | **The Dead as Cultural Heritage.** The law and the dead. Can we own the dead? Exhuming, selling, displaying and repatriating the dead |
| 7 | **Creating Portable Heritage in Hong Kong.** Streets and graffiti artists and their legal rights. Street art and graffiti as cultural heritage in Hong Kong. The “King of Kowloon”. Can you own graffiti? *The multiple choice (MC) test will be administered in week 7, as a mid-term assessment with 20 questions. The results will be provided to students within one week of taking the test to allow them to consider their engagement and progress in the course.* |
| 8 | **Cultural Heritage and Digital Technology.** The use of blockchain tokenisation and artificial intelligence in the creation and safeguarding of culture heritage in Hong Kong. The issues with and law affecting digital technology and cultural heritage including owning buying, selling and giving tokens of real and virtual art, and intellectual property law issues for art generated by artificial intelligence. |
| 9 | **Hong Kong’s Part in the Illicit Trade in Antiquities from China.** The legal framework affecting antiquities in China- focus on the Greater Bay Area (GBA) and possible harmonization of the law to safeguard China’s cultural heritage from illicit excavation and trafficking. |
| 10 | **Hong Kong’s Water Heritage.** The law affecting searching for, owning, buying and selling shipwrecks and treasure. The legal safeguarding framework for Hong Kong’s water heritage |
| 11 | **What is Intangible Cultural Heritage?** International and domestic laws affecting intangible cultural heritage. The Convention for Safeguarding of the Intangible Cultural Heritage (2003). China’s championing of intangible cultural heritage. China’s rich and varied intangible cultural heritage. |
| 12 | **Safeguarding Intangible Cultural Heritage in Hong Kong.** 2008 and Hong Kong’s adoption of intangible culture heritage. The legal recognition and safeguarding of intangible cultural heritage in Hong Kong. Hong Kong’s officially recognised intangible cultural heritage: fire dragons, Chinese medicine, Cantonese opera, bamboo theatres, sek pun (basin feast) and milk tea. Hong Kong’s unofficial intangible culture heritage. Issues with intangible cultural heritage including cultural appropriation. |
| 13 | **Review and Conclusions.** The future for safeguarding cultural heritage in Hong Kong. Possible devleopemsnt in domestic laws in Hong Kong which may affect cultural heritage, and the extension of international legal measures to Hong Kong. |

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: History of Women in Modern China
courses.description: Gender inequalities often stem from distinctions either empirically grounded or socially constructed. “History” is of essential importance in understanding how these inequalities are constructed in different periods and different contexts. This course traces and examines the history of women in China from the late Qing period to the present. Discover and analyze the sex roles, sexual politics and sexual symbolism in the course of modernization and nation building, how they are related to economic development, ideological shifts and colonialism, and to find out how they functioned to reduce gender distinctions (SDG#10 Reduced Inequalities), how they affected the formulation and changes of women policy (SDG#16 Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions), and how they promoted woman’s rights and social changes (SDG#5 Gender Equality).
courses.enrollment_requirement:
Not for History Majors.
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.course_attributes:
SDG-GE #5 Gender Equality
SDG-GE #10 Reducing Inequality
SDG-GE #16 Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Have a better understanding of the history of modern China in a gendered perspective.
2. Develop deeper understanding of women’s rights movement in modern China, and the roles and contributions of women in the evolution and development of Chinese society.
3. Understanding how the women policy in modern China is formulated (SDG#16 Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions) and changed over times in promoting the rights of women (SDG#10 Reduced Inequalities)
4. Recognize and critically analyze how and why various contexts shaping the lives and experience of women in modern China.
5. Cultivate sensitivity and fair evaluation to issues related to sexuality and gender.
6. Evaluate and reflect on how to promote gender equality (SDG#5 Gender Equality) based on the experience of the past.
courses.course_syllabus:
(1) Introduction

* What is Feminism?
* How the Feminist Movement in the West Affecting Modern China?
* The Importance of Women History in Gender Studies

(2) Chinese Cultural Traditions and Women in History

* Female Inferiority Ideas – How It Emerged and Evolved in Chinese History?
* Confucian Rituals and Values Suppressed Women?
* An Overview of the Lives of Women in Traditional China

(3) Women’s Emancipation: Anti-Footbinding

* Origin and Tradition of Footbinding
* Different Voices in the Anti-Footbining Movement: Late Qing’s Reformers and the Missionaries
* Relationship between the Anti-Footbinding Campaigns and the Building of A “Modern State”

(4) Women’s Emancipation: Women Education

* “Educating the Women” and Its Relation to “Building A Strong Nation”
* New Wine in Old Bottles? – Development of Women Education During the Late Qing Reforms
* Missionaries and Women University Education

(5) Modern Girls in Shanghai

* “Modern Girls”: Constructing A New Image for Women
* The Two Dimensions of Capitalism: Working Woman and Foreign Goods Consumption
* Critiques and Receptions Towards “Modern Girls”

(6) Love and Sexuality of Republican Women

(7) Women and Revolution

(8) Women in Mao’s China

* Women’s Policy in Mao’s China

* The Life and Experience of “Iron Girls” in Rural China

(9) Women in China’s Economic Reform

* Chinese State Capitalism: The South China Regional Economy

* Gender Politics, Prejudice and Discrimination in Workplace

(10) Sworn Spinsters in Southern China

* Marriage Resistance and Alternative Marriage Patterns in South China
* Sworn Sisterhood: Their Customs and Social Identity
* The Historical and Cultural Significance of the Sworn Spinsters

(11) Hong Kong Women: *Mui Tsai*

* The *Mui Tsai* System and the Problem of Female Trafficking in Colonial Hong Kong

(12) Hong Kong Women: Factory Girls

* Hong Kong’s Post-war Industrialization and the Female Factory Workers
* The Image of “Factory Girls” in Hong Kong’s Popular Culture
* Between Economic Independence and Family Responsibility

(13) Conclusion

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Cities of the World: A Cultural and Historical Exploration of Urban Development
courses.description: This course is designed to offer an exhaustive and nuanced exploration of the intricate patterns and rich historical tapestries of urban development worldwide, viewed through a geographic lens. Throughout this course, students will be introduced to a wide range of theories and concepts that are intricately linked to the social and economic evolution of cities across different world regions. These theories and concepts include colonialism, postcolonialism, capitalism, neoliberalism, globalization, post-industrial development, urbanization, and urban sprawl. By exploring these theories and concepts, students will gain a comprehensive understanding of urban development within diverse geographic contexts and with varying historical backgrounds. This course is relevant to SDGs 10 (Reduced Inequalities) and 11 (Sustainable Cities and Communities). It will provide students with the knowledge and tools to identify inequalities in urban development and analyze the challenges and opportunities in achieving sustainable cities and communities. The curriculum is thoughtfully divided into five key sections, each providing an in-depth analysis of urban development in different regions – Europe, America, Sub-Saharan Africa, the Greater Middle East, and Asia. These sections are carefully curated to help students gain a profound understanding of the diverse economic, political, cultural, and historical influences that have shaped and continue to shape urban landscapes in these varied regions. Students will engage with diverse learning methodologies. This includes detailed case studies that provide real-world examples of urban development, scholarly readings that offer academic perspectives, visual aids that illustrate key concepts, and interactive discussions that encourage critical thinking and collaborative learning. Ideal for students with a passion for urban studies, geography, history, sociology, or related disciplines, this course offers a unique opportunity to explore the world of urban development in a global context, preparing them to become informed and engaged global citizens.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
Not for Geography and Resource Management Majors.
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix (only applicable to students admitted to the 4-year curriculum)
courses.course_attributes:
SDG-GE #10 Reducing Inequality
SDG-GE #11 Sustainable Cities and Communities
courses.learning_outcomes:
Students are expected to delve deeply into the multifaceted histories and cultures of cities around the world in this course. This academic journey equips them with the skills to analyze the impact of economic, political, cultural, and historical influences on urban development. They will learn to compare and contrast the unique paths and common patterns in the development of different cities, enhancing their understanding of global urban evolution. The course aims not just to impart knowledge, but also to sharpen critical thinking and research skills, fostering a deep appreciation for the intricate and diverse nature of cities. After completing the course, students will be able to:

* Explore the diverse historical and cultural narratives of major world cities.
* Investigate the impact of significant historical events and cultural and religious movements on urban development.
* Compare and contrast the unique characteristics, urban patterns, and challenges of various cities.
* Analyze the role of urban planning in shaping city identities.
* Develop critical thinking skills through a geographic lens.
* Foster a global perspective by understanding the interconnectedness of cities worldwide.
* Understand contemporary issues, challenges, and opportunities in achieving equal and sustainable cities and communities.
courses.course_syllabus:
As shown below, the course contents will be divided into 5 parts and 12 topics.

Part I: World urban development (1 lecture)

The course begins with a singular, foundational lecture that sets the stage for understanding global urban development. It introduces key concepts, trends, and the factors driving urbanization worldwide, providing a global context for the more detailed regional studies that follow.

1. World urban development

世界城市發展

Part II: Europe (3 lectures)

The European segment consists of three lectures. The first lecture focuses on the development of European cities from ancient times to modernity, highlighting the impacts of technological, social, and economic development on the evolution of urban landscapes and patterns. The second lecture takes students on a journey to explore distinctive cities across Europe, examining the unique characteristics that define them. In particular, we will discuss the issue of camera surveillance in London, the refugee crisis in Paris, post-socialist development in Berlin, and superblocks and walkability in Barcelona. The final lecture in this section shifts focus to Russia, discussing both historical and contemporary aspects of Russian urban development, and offering insights into the evolution of its cities amidst political and social changes. Students will learn about the unique urban landscape of Russian cities inherited from their imperial and Soviet histories and discuss issues such as the environmental crisis of Norilsk and the problem of street children in post-Soviet cities.

2. Historical perspectives on urban development in Europe

歐洲城市發展的歷史觀察

3. Exploring distinctive cities in Europe

探索歐洲特色城市

4. Historical and contemporary Russian cities

俄羅斯城市的歷史與現狀

Part III: America (3 lectures)

This section, also encompassing three lectures, begins by delving into the history of urban development in Latin America. It examines the colonial impact, post-colonial developments, and contemporary challenges. The subsequent lecture takes students on a virtual tour of unique Latin American cities, exploring their cultural, architectural, and social nuances. We will discuss the issues of favelas in Rio de Janeiro, the impact of modernism on urban design and architecture in Brasilia, and gated communities in Buenos Aires. The last lecture of this section examines North American cities, discussing issues such as racial and social segregation in New York City, the development of shared mobility and sustainable transportation systems in Portland, auto dependence and urban sprawl in Los Angeles, and post-industrial development and gentrification in Toronto.

5. Understanding the history of Latin American urban development

解讀拉丁美洲城市發展的歷史

6. A journey to unique Latin American cities

造訪獨特的拉丁美洲城市

7. Historical overview and distinctive cities in the United States and Canada

美國和加拿大的歷史概述與獨特城市

Part IV: Sub-Saharan Africa and the Greater Middle East (3 lectures)

Three lectures are devoted to these regions. The first concerns urban development in Sub-Saharan Africa, discussing issues like post-colonial race and class segregation in Johannesburg, squatter settlements in Nairobi, and inequality in Lagos. The second lecture turns to the Greater Middle East, exploring the historical evolution of cities under the influence of Islamic culture, colonial history, the independence movement, and contemporary conflicts. The third lecture invites students to virtually visit distinctive cities in the Greater Middle East, investigating issues such as the city of the dead and the informal economy in Cairo, religious tourism in Mecca, green infrastructure in Dubai, and Palestinian-Israeli conflict in Jerusalem.

8. Urban development and distinctive cities in Sub-Saharan Africa

撒哈拉以南非洲的城市發展與獨特城市

9. Historical evolution of urban development in the Greater Middle East

大中東地區城市發展的歷史演變

10. Visiting distinctive cities in the Greater Middle East

探訪大中東地區特色城市

Part V: Asia (2 lectures)

The course concludes with two lectures on Asia. The first examines the historical geography of urban development in South and Southeast Asia, focusing on the influences of colonialism, religious and ethnic conflicts, and territorial disputes. This lecture will examine issues such as air pollution in Delhi, the climate migration crisis in Dhaka, and Singapore’s strategies for smart city development and public housing management. The final lecture focuses on East Asian cities, analyzing their past and present, and how traditions and modernity coexist and interact in these urban centers. Issues that will be discussed in this lecture include urban congestion in Beijing, population aging in Tokyo, and the influence of the declining birthrate and pop culture on urban fabrics in Seoul.

11. Historical geography of urban development in South and Southeast Asia

南亞和東南亞城市發展的歷史地理

12. The past and present of East Asian cities

東亞城市的過去與現在

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Decolonizing Classical Music
courses.description:
The universal appeal of classical music is masked by a troubled past. It is a complex story of white colonization by European powers, spanning centuries and continents. Classical music has its own colonial baggage, too, with links to slavery profits and other problematic phenomena, whether in the gendered tradition of the “great composers,” or in a thriving piano industry of the early 20th century, which resulted in forced labor and countless deaths in the colonial ivory trade, or in the transnational circulation of opera in and beyond the 19th century, which would not have been possible without the port city networks of rapidly expanding empires.

Importantly, such legacies have led to new forms of advocacy aimed at decolonizing classical music today. This SDG-GE course will examine how current practices around the world, on the back of complex and violent histories, attempt to democratize classical music in lockstep with the needs and concerns of contemporary society. What does it mean to decolonize musically in the 21st century, recenter marginalized individuals and groups in classical music, and make classical music more sustainable and reflective of a fair society? These questions run through the three themed sections of the syllabus: i) gender and representation, ii) equality, diversity, inclusion, iii) institutions and justice. We will explore related ideas and concepts, in connection with past contexts and present practices, including orchestras, opera companies, community projects, and social enterprises. By drawing on a variety of perspectives, we will discuss how and why decolonizing classical music matters, to whom, along with its ongoing challenges for improving and enhancing equity.

This course engages directly with SDGs 5, 10, and 16. Through the three themed sections, we will assess how classical music practices today can be observed to respond to the identified SDGs, particularly in terms of further empowering indigenous women musicians, reducing age-old inequalities, and promoting greater social justice from within institutions that can better reflect the untold tales and human experiences of classical music.

Neither score-reading nor prior musical knowledge is required for this course.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
Not for Music Majors. 
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.course_attributes:
SDG-GE #5 Gender Equality
SDG-GE #10 Reducing Inequality
SDG-GE #16 Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions
courses.learning_outcomes:
* Develop an enriched understanding of decolonizing classical music today.
* Demonstrate awareness of, and engagement with, introductory questions and readings that are directly concerned with, or topically relevant to, the subject of democratizing classical music's role in contemporary society.
* Examine and discuss the state of classical music in connection with past contexts and current practices.
* Analyze ongoing developments and challenges in classical music in relation to the highlighted topics of gender and representation (SDG 5), equality, diversity, inclusion (SDG 10), and institutions and justice (SDG 16).
courses.course_syllabus:
Course objective: To develop an enriched understanding of decolonizing classical music today, by examining how current practices around the world, on the back of complex and violent histories, attempt to democratize classical music in lockstep with the needs and concerns of contemporary society.

I: Gender and representation

The first section of the course engages with SDG 5. Students will explore the topic of representation and related contexts and practices in decolonizing classical music, as well as efforts to address issues of gender and race, visibility, and difference through new forms of advocacy.

* “The white-male canon”: decolonizing musically in the 21st century

Session goal: To introduce the idea of decolonization, together with the phenomenon of a gendered and racialized musical canon, both in the historical contexts of European colonization around the world, and in dialogue with decolonizing classical music today across university education and the performing arts.

* Indigenous women in classical music, then and now

Session goal: To consider the visibility of indigenous women in classical music, by examining the impact of colonial oppression on their prospects and mobility, along with present challenges and opportunities for their artistic and social empowerment.

* Difference

Session goal: To explore the work of queer and non-binary musicians and organizations today, and its significance for articulating difference in classical music and moving on from its colonial past.

II: Equality, diversity, inclusion (EDI)

The second section of the course engages with SDG 10. Students will explore the topic of EDI and related contexts and practices in decolonizing classical music, as well as efforts to reduce systemic, distributive, and discriminatory inequalities through new forms of advocacy.

* EDI in classical music: recentering the marginalized

Session goal: To examine the role marginalized voices play in advancing new community projects, social enterprises, and other grassroot initiatives in the decolonization of classical music, with a focus on systemic inequalities stemming from the legacies of music and empire.

* EDI in classical music: “de-westernization”

Session goal: To explore the function of regional and local identities in the decolonization and “de-westernization” of classical music, with a focus on distributive inequalities tied to age-old perceptions of “the West and the rest.”

* EDI in classical music: creative disability

Session goal: To understand how disabled artists, disability service organizations, and adaptive technologies contribute to creativity disability in the decolonization of classical music, and against an historical backdrop of discriminate inequalities, shaped by Victorian pathological distinctions of “normal” and “abnormal.”

III: Institutions and justice

The third and final section of the course engages with SDG 16. Students will explore the topic of social justice and related contexts and practices in decolonizing classical music, as well as institutional efforts at making classical music more sustainable and reflective of a fair society.

* Orchestras in the 21st century

Session goal: To explore the orchestra as a decolonising force and public institution, particularly the ways and the extent to which it augments social justice and participation through classical music of the 21st century.

* Opera companies in the 21st century

Session goal: To explore the opera company as a decolonising force and public institution, particularly the ways and the extent to which it augments social justice and participation through classical music of the 21st century.

courses.description:
(UGEC2171 is double-coded with GRMD2401.)

Sustainable development is a global vision for the future. This approach to development should not only generate economic growth, but also distribute its benefits equitably, regenerate the environment rather than destroy it, as well as empower people rather than marginalize them.

The UN 2030 Agenda came into effect in 2015 promising to “interlink and integrate” Sustainable Development Goals in an attempt to implement solutions facing humanity. Sustainable development goals are an all-inclusive invitation to act to end poverty, secure the planet and ensure that all people appreciate peace and prosperity. In this course, the selected SDGs include SDG 1 (No Poverty), SDG 2 (Zero Hunger), SDG 3 (Good Health and Well-being), SDG 6 (Clean Water and Sanitation), SDG 7 (Affordable and Clean Energy), SDG 8 (Decent Work and Economic Growth), SDG 9 (Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure), SDG 10 (Reduced Inequalities), SDG 12 (Responsible Consumption and Production), SDG 13 (Climate Action), SDG 14 (Life Below Water) and SDG 15 (Life on Land), which together cover inclusive development, human well-being, resource security, low-carbon transitions, sustainable production, and ecosystem protection.

This course introduces the concept of sustainable development, the evolution of models and the implicit assumptions they make, as well as their contradictions. It also traces the advent of the SDGs and the extent that they support the principles of sustainable development. We examine the emergence of environmental problems and critically review the institutional responses to these problems since the late 1960s. We then consider in detail the increasingly popular idea of efforts in the pursuit of the SDGs from different perspectives that centres around people, planet, prosperity, peace and the partnerships involved in achieving the goals. The lectures will explore how achieving sustainable development goals will depends much on effective planning and good governance. Examples from different parts of the world will be used to underline the tight inter-connections between processes in different regions and across geographical scales in the context of sustainable development.

courses.course_syllabus:
1. Introduction to Sustainable Development: Origin and Evolution

(To introduce why we need sustainable development, the history of sustainable development, and Sustainable Development Goals.)

2. Planetary Boundary (I) (SDG3 – Good Health and Well-being, SDG 13 – Climate Action, SDG 14 – Life Below Water, SDG 15 – Life on Land)

(To introduce the basic concept of five planetary boundaries, including Climate Change, Stratospheric Ozone Depletion, Ocean Acidification, Atmospheric Aerosol Loadings, and Freshwater Use.)

3. Planetary Boundary (II) (SDG 13 – Climate Action, SDG 14 – Life Below Water, SDG 15 – Life on Land)

(To introduce the basic concept of another four planetary boundaries, including Biogeochemical Flows, Novel Entities, Land System Change, and Biosphere Integrity.)

4. Ecosystem Services, Ecological Footprint and Carbon Footprint (SDG 13 – Climate Action, SDG 15 – Life on Land)

(To introduce the four important ecosystem services provided by the natural environment; to show how to perform simple ecological footprint and carbon footprint evaluation.)

5. Sustainable agriculture and food system (SDG 1 – No Poverty, SDG 2 – Zero Hunger, SDG 6 – Clean Water and Sanitation)

(To introduce methods in sustainable agriculture, such as hydroponics and aquaponics, and to introduce sustainable food systems in urban areas.)

6. Policy for Sustainable Development (SDG 13 – Climate Action)

(To introduce different types of policy tools, such as market-based approaches and voluntary agreements.)

7: Sustainable Development Goals and Net-Zero transitions

(SDG 7–Affordable and Clean Energy, SDG 13 – Climate Action)

(To introduce contemporary and future directions for sustainability policy.)

8: Green Growth Strategies for Sustainable Development

(SDG 8–Decent Work and Economic Growth, SDG 9–Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure)

(To introduce key green industries, such as battery electric vehicles or renewables, and understand how they are changing economic development opportunities – highlighting perspectives that we can innovate and ‘grow’ our way out of the climate crisis without reducing consumption.)

9: The Circular Economy

(SDG 12–Responsible Consumption and Production)

(To introduce the circular economy model as opposed to the linear model – highlighting perspectives that economic growth is fundamentally unsustainable and we must reduce our material footprint and consumption.)

10: Just Transition Strategies for Sustainable Development

(SDG 10–Reduced Inequalities)

(To introduce elements of sustainable development that focus on socio-cultural dynamics and inequalities – highlighting perspectives that emphasize the socio-cultural consequences of sustainable development.)

11: Sustainable Finance (I)

(SDG 8–Decent Work and Economic Growth)

(To introduce the important role that finance plays in funding sustainable development. Particularly, greenwashing will be a focus.)

12: Sustainable Finance (II)

(SDG 8–Decent Work and Economic Growth)

(To introduce and assess whether several new ‘green’ funding possibilities can make a significant difference or are likely to be more greenwashing.)

13: Group Presentation

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Hong Kong English and its Culture
courses.description:
(UGEC2188 is double-coded with ENGE2530.)

The objective of this course is to give students an overview of the status, features, and use of Hong Kong English (HKE), the variety of English commonly used in Hong Kong. In the first part of the course, students will be introduced to the concept of ‘world Englishes’ and examine different theoretical frameworks for conceptualizing the evolution of new varieties of English. The course then examines relationship between Hong Kong English and Hong Kong culture from cultural, social, historical, and educational perspectives, particularly in relation to and juxtaposition from Cantonese and Putonghua. The second part of the course examines both spoken and written features of HKE, including grammar, discourse particles, vocabulary, and pronunciation, as well as the practices of code-mixing and code-switching. This section of the course also focuses on the social impact of the use of these features in Hong Kong culture. In the third part of the course, language attitudes and ideologies towards ‘standard’ language varieties (for example American and British English) in relation to HKE will be explored. The course will also examine the relationship between use of HKE and social identity as well as gender in Hong Kong culture.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGEC2188 is double-coded with ENGE2530. 
Not for English; English Studies and English Language Education (ELED) Majors; and students who have taken ENGE2530. 
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.course_syllabus:
Week 1

• Introduction: syllabus, reading material, teaching method

The first week will introduce students to the course and the course syllabus.

• No reading

Week 2

• Topic: New varieties of English

In week 2, the ‘how’ of Hong Kong English will be discussed, and key frameworks to analyze the evolution of new Englishes will be examined.

* *•** Reading: Hansen Edwards 2018 Chapter 2

Week 3

• Topic: A social and linguistic history of English in Hong Kong

In week 3, the ‘how’ of Hong Kong English will continued to be examined through the presentation of a social, cultural, and linguistic history of Hong Kong, which emphasizes the history and status of English in Hong Kong society in juxtaposition to both Cantonese and Putonghua.

* *•** Reading: Hansen Edwards 2018 Chapter 1; Chan, 2002; Evans, 2017

Week 4

• Topic: What’s in a name? Chinglish, Kongish, and Hong Kong English

In week 4, the course will focus on the ‘what’ of Hong Kong English. It will examine and differentiate among three terms often used to describe the English of Hong Kong: Kongish, Chinglish, and Hong Kong English, particular in terms of the social meanings of these terms in Hong Kong culture. These terms will be explored against the frameworks discussed in week 2.

* *•** Reading: Bolton, 2002; Leung, 2015

Week 5

• Topic: Features of HKE – Pronunciation

Week 5 continues the focus on the ‘what’ of HKE through a discussion of the pronunciation features of HKE. This week also explores the social impact of use of these features in Hong Kong society.

* *•** Reading: Hansen Edwards 2018 Chapter 7; Deterding, Wong, and Kirkpatrick, 2008

Week 6

• Topic: Features of HKE – Grammar

Week 6 continues the examination of features of Hong Kong English by examining the grammar of HKE. This week also explores the social impact of use of these features in Hong Kong society.

* *•** Reading: Hansen Edwards 2018 Chapter 7; Sung, 2015

Week 7

• Topic: Features of HKE – Vocabulary

Week 7 focuses on the vocabulary (including discourse particles) of HKE. This week also explores the social impact of use of these features in Hong Kong society.

• Reading: Hansen Edwards 2018 Chapter 7; *New Hong Kong English Words (OED)*

Week 8

• Topic: Written and spoken HKE

In week 8, the ‘when’ of HKE will be explored, particularly in relation to spoken vs. written language as well as code-mixing and code-switching. This week also explores the social meaning of code-switching and code-mixing in Hong Kong culture.

* *•** Reading: Li, 2000; Sung, 2015

Week 9

• Topic: Attitudes towards HKE

In week 9, students will first be introduced to the concepts of ‘language attitudes’ and ‘standard language ideologies’. Attitudes towards HKE both locally and globally will then be examined, particularly in relation to ‘standard’ varieties of English such as American and British English. This essentially explores the ‘why’ of HKE.

•Reading: Hansen Edwards 2018 Chapters 2, 5, 6; Chan, 2016

Week 10

• Topic: HKE and social identity

In week 10, the ‘who’ of HKE will be explored as the discussion focuses on the use of HKE as a social identity marker.

* *•**Reading: Hansen Edwards 2018 Chapters 3 & 6

Week 11

• Topic: HKE and gender

Week 11 continues the exploration of the ‘who’ of HKE through the examination of gender and HKE.

* *•** Reading: Hansen Edwards 2018 Chapter 6

Week 12

• Topic: HKE in social media

In week 12, the use of Hong Kong English in social media, including on Facebook and YouTube and by artists such as GYOBN, will be explored. This discussion will also revisit the use of the terms Kongish and Chinglish, and their use by social media in relation to HKE.

* *•** Reading: Chan, 2015; Li, 2015; Yau, 2016

Week 12

• Topic: Conclusions and revisions

In the final week of the course, a revision of the course content will conducted and final conclusions will be drawn about Hong Kong English.

* *•** No reading

courses.description:
(UGEC2189 is double-coded with ENGE2600.)

Although English has long spread around the world, the consequences of this spread are only now beginning to be appreciated by speakers in diverse cultures and societies. English has changed the world, impacting specific local cultures in both positive and negative ways. But English has also been localized and acculturated, made to express the realities of diverse cultures and societies, and so the language has been changed in turn. The discourse of world Englishes aims to re-imagine our understanding of the language. The difference between error and innovation can no longer be decided by reference to ‘ownership’ of the language. Additionally, the language is beginning to be a medium of the expression of identity for more and more people in very different social and cultural contexts. World English must be pluralized, which is why we think in terms of world Englishes. This course is split into two sections. The first section will introduce you to issues in the discourse of world Englishes: innovation, variety, creativity, globalization, etc. The second section will look at specific case studies in different cultures and societies, and will focus on Asian Englishes, e.g. English in Singapore, China, and Japan. Different Asian cultures have distinct historical experiences with English, leading to different levels of cultural penetration (e.g. in popular culture) and depth of function (e.g. in business and law). The second section looks at different examples to explore their specific cultural engagements with the English language, but also to identify what they have in common: Japan English and Singlish develop in very different cultures, but the course with emphasis comparison as well as contrast.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGEC2189 is double-coded with ENGE2600. 
Not for English; English Studies and English Language Education (ELED) Majors; and students who have taken ENGE2600. 
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.course_syllabus:
* introduces popular ideas and linguistic theories about World Englishes;

* contextualizes examples of World Englishes, especially in Asian cultures and societies;

* builds vocabulary and demonstrates different methods for study in linguistics and culture;

* develops a range of study skills relevant to further undergraduate studies in general.

The main topics and regions to be covered are as follows, along with required reading and issues raised:

Introduction: McWhorter, ‘The cosmopolitan tongue’, *World Affairs Journal* Vol. 172, No. 2 (2009), pp.61-68.

In this week the course will raise general questions about the utility and desirability of a global language. Many have celebrated English as a global language, and the course will consider both positive and negative possibilities that will be investigated using specific examples later in the term.

Histories of English: Crystal, ‘Why a Global Language?’, from *English as a Global Language* (Cambridge University Press, 2003), pp.1-27.

This week looks back to some of the important histories of how English became a global language, particularly the connections with capitalism and colonialism. It will explore the charge that English today is a form of linguistic imperialism.

Mistakes and Creativity: Quirk, ‘Language varieties and standard language’, *English Today* 21 (1990), pp.3-10.

What do people look for when learning a language? This week wonders why, if we would never imagine World Russians or World Chineses, it seems important to accept World Englishes. We will ask if English still has clearly defined centres of authority, or whether it is now dispersed and plural.

Standards: Modiano, ‘International English in the Global Village’, *English Today* 58 (1999), pp.22-27.

This week’s discussion will focus on the need for standards, and question whether our standards should come from native speakers. Many aspects of international life (scientific discourse, aviation) currently depend on English, but perhaps native speakers are not well placed to help develop these standards. We will think about why that might be true, and how we should test Englishes in future.

English in Asia: Kachru, ‘English as an Asian Language’, *Links and Letters* 5 (1998), pp.89-108.

The focus of the course shifts this week to Asia as a specific example (or series of examples) of the World Englishes. In some ways, English does not appear to belong in Asia, and yet it has clearly been accepted, and we will explore the different ways English functions and has penetrated cultural life.

Singapore: Ho, ‘“I’m not west. I’m not east. So how leh?”’, *English Today* 87 (2006), pp.17-24.

Singapore allows us to think about the general issues clearly because it is a restricted example. Everyone is familiar with Singlish, but what exactly does Singlish show us about the cultural life and social development of this relatively young city-state? The week examines different and sometimes contradictory aspects of English in Singapore.

Japan: Stanlaw, ‘The dynamics of English words in contemporary Japanese’, from *Japan English* (Hong Kong University Press, 2004), pp.11-37.

Shifting attention from former British colonies, this week explores Japan as an example where English seems to depend on another native speaker country, the United States. However, if we ask how Japanese society and culture actually uses English, far from being dependent on native speaker standards, we see independent creativity.

China: Hu, ‘Why China English should stand alongside British, American, and the other “World Englishes”’, *English Today* 78 (2004), pp.26-33.

Given the rise of China, it is obvious to ask, does China even need English? And does it need a specific China English? Exploring varied examples, this week considers the long history of English in China and some possible futures, in particular of Putonghua alongside English.

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Understanding Ecotourism
courses.description:
(UGEC2192 is double-coded with GRMD2292.)
The first part of the course will cover the general introductory concepts and provide students with theoretical and historical background of the discipline. This will be followed by in-depth study of specific environmental settings and resource issues that will complement our understanding of ecotourism. Hong Kong is the main focus of discussion though examples or cases in Mainland China and around the world are also provided throughout the course. Critical issues in ecotourism such as its multi-dimensional impacts, governance, planning, and resource and visitor management will also be covered. Lastly, students will be exposed to the relation of the phenomenon of ecotourism and the concept of sustainable tourism. Some of the lectures will address SDGs, for example, Goals 12, 16 and 17 embrace the knowledge and evolution of environmentalism and nature-human relationship; Goals 14 and 15 connect with the concept of protected areas and environmental conservation; Goals 1, 3, 5, 8, 11, 12 and 13 cover multi-dimensional tourism impacts and, more importantly, climate change issue. The concept of sustainable development will frame and conclude the entire course since the concepts has played an indispensable role in tourism development nowadays.

UGEC2192 is double-coded with GRMD2292.
Not for Geography and Resource Management Majors; and students who have taken GRMD2292. 
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix (only applicable to students admitted to the 4-year curriculum)
courses.course_attributes:
SDG-GE #12 Responsible Consumption and Production
SDG-GE #14 Life Below Water
SDG-GE #15 Life On Land
Experiential Learning Course
courses.learning_outcomes:
| This course will provide a broad intellectual perspective for students and enable them to develop a holistic understanding of environmental and resource management issues related to ecotourism. Students will acquire basic knowledge of ecotourism development and its potential and can be capable of making judgment in response to critical issues on conservation and natural resource management. Since ecotourism, sustainable tourism and sustainable development (including SDGs) are inter-connected concepts in theoretical and practical perspectives, students are expected to incorporate and integrate these areas of knowledge with various cases and examples provided in the course. Specifically, after successfully completing this course, students will be able to: - Understand the basic concepts of ecotourism and its relation with the overall tourism industry; especially connecting ecotourism with sustainable tourism development; - Acquire knowledge in operating and managing ecotourism activities; and particularly how these ecotourism activities may impact economic, socio-cultural and ecological/environmental sustainability; - Develop an understanding and the skills and approaches necessary for interpreting relevant policy and planning of natural and man-made resources for (eco)tourism; and - Enhance problem-solving capability using an integrative approach and critical thinking as introduced in the course, which draws students’ attention to how tourism and ecotourism may contribute to SDGs potentially |
| --- |
courses.course_syllabus:
| This course includes the following main topics of teaching: 1) Ecotourism: An introduction (What is tourism, ecotourism and sustainable development?) *(SDG 12)* 2) The ideology of ecotourism and environmental conservation (How does ecotourism connect with environmentalism and sustainability?) *(SDGs 16, 17)* 3) Who are the ecotourists? (What is an ecotourist? Does the ecotourism market sustainable?) *(SDG 12)* 4) Ecotourism environments (What and where may ecotourism take place? How to protect these resources for ecotourism and sustainable tourism development?) *(SDGs 14, 15)* 5) Ecotourism in Hong Kong: Potential and limitations (What is status of ecotourism development in Hong Kong?) *(SDGs 8, 11, 14, 15)* 6) Geological landscape in Hong Kong as tourism resources (What is the Hong Kong UNESCO Global Geopark? How does the Geopark provide opportunity for ecotourism in Hong Kong?) *(SDGs 11, 14, 15)* 7) Ecotourism in Mainland China: Potential and limitations (What is status of ecotourism development in mainland China?) *(SDGs 1, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12)* 8) Impacts of ecotourism (What are the multi-dimensional impacts of tourism and ecotourism? How do these impacts affect the sustainability of ecotourism destinations?) *(SDGs 1, 3, 5, 8, 11, 12)* 9) Ecotourism planning and management (How can we plan and manage ecotourism effectively to achieve sustainable tourism development?) *(SDGs 1, 5, 8, 9, 10, 11, 16, 17)* 10) Conclusion: Ecotourism and sustainable tourism (How does climate change affect ecotourism and sustainable tourism development? What would be the possible future of tourism?) *(SDGs 1, 3, 5, 8, 11, 12, 13)* |
| --- |

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Pursuit of Ideal Living Environment
courses.description:
The pursuit of ideal living environment has long been a human undertaking throughout history. Because of environmental variations, some places are definitely better than the others for living and development. In ancient time, man strived for searching a nice place for settlement where could provide a better resource base. Nowadays, man acquires sciences and technologies to change or even create the environment that makes living pleasant and comfortable. Those human settlements which develop an integrated and sustainable approach to the environmental, economic and social challenges will have more chance to grow and prosper.

Ideal living environment is closely related with several Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), particularly SDG11 and SDG13 with topics covered in this course. Sustainable cities and communities (SDG 11) directly address the need for safe, inclusive, and resilient living environments. It includes targets for affordable housing, sustainable urban planning, and access to green and public spaces, resilient building.

Climate Action (SDG 13) mitigates climate change impacts through sustainable practices which helps protect living environments from extreme weather events and environmental degradation.

By focusing on these related SDGs, students can create their ideal living environments by promoting sustainability, enhancing quality of life and addressing climate change challenges that not only meet the needs of current communities that are inclusive, resilient and environmentally friendly, but also ensure sustainability for future generations.

This course provides students with the insights needed to gain an understanding of ideal environment for living from the perspective of a geographer. Key themes include searching for the best location, the rise of city, forms of human settlement, ideal living and quality of life. As the course examines the understanding of ideal environment for living from different angles, students will not only gain objective geographical knowledge about the relationship between man and environment, but also learn how some urban challenges and issues are tackled. Ecological civilisation, landscape cities and sustainable development of ideal living environment will also be explored. While the coverage will be broad and pluralistic, examples and cases of Hong Kong, mainland China, and other parts of the world will be emphasized.

In order to enhance the learning experience, an interactive teaching mode will be adopted in the lectures so that the instructor can guide students to analyse the cases and issues through discussions.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
Not for Geography and Resource Management Majors; and students who have taken UGEC2775.
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.course_attributes:
SDG-GE #11 Sustainable Cities and Communities
SDG-GE #13 Climate Action
courses.course_syllabus:
This course portraits an image of ideal living environment from a geographical perspective. Specifically, the following topics under four themes are covered:

THEME 1: SEARCHING FOR THE BEST LOCATION

1. What is an ideal living environment?

This lecture explores where the best living place is on Earth, why is there uneven population and the criteria of an “ideal living environment”.

2. A history of human settlements

This lecture explores how early humans lived, when and where did human migration begin, how were human settlements evolved, why cities emerged and the history of human settlement in Hong Kong.

3. Environmental perception: Components of an “ideal” environment

This lecture explores what is civilisation, where did civilisation take place and why, what were the favourable geographical settings of the ancient capitals and the effectiveness of ‘*Feng shui*’.

THEME 2: THE RISE OF CITY

4. Cities in the world, urbanization and cityscape

This lecture explores the concepts of city and urbanisation, the benefits and problems of cities and their identity.

5. A sustainable city? Climate adaptation and future-proofing a city

This lecture explores the concepts of sustainable development, sustainable cities, climate change and how to live with it.

6. Urban planning and land use: A geographical approach

This lecture explores the basics and rationales of urban planning, and how to plan sustainable cities with case studies.

THEME 3: FORMS OF HUMAN SETTLEMENT

7. Architecture: The interface between man and environment

This lecture explores the basics of architecture, the styles of architecture, the differences between western and Chinese architecture.

8. Interior design: Home sweet home

This lecture explores the basics of interior design, the decision-making process, the elements and principles of interior design and the popular design styles.

9. Garden: A simulation of natural environment

This lecture explores the main types and systems of gardens in the world, the development of Chinese-style gardens, and modern-style gardens.

10. Grave: Home for afterlife

This lecture explores the definition of death, afterlife, rituals and graves as home for afterlife.

THEME 4: IDEAL LIVING AND QUALITY OF LIFE

11. Ideal living and quality of life in Chinese context

This lecture introduces the sciences of human settlements in Chinese context, the five key elements of living environment, and the four stages of city development perspective.

12. Liveability and migration intention

This lecture discusses the most liveable city on Earth, the latest findings of liveable city and sharing experience of overseas living.

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Decoding Cities
courses.description:
Cities have always been magnets attracting a growing number of citizens. The unprecedented pace of urbanization has recently witnessed a historic shift of human population with the majority now living in cities. The growth of cities in this age of globalization raises many opportunities and challenges, concerning the quality of life, poverty, sustainability, environmental degradation and urban competitiveness. All of which deserve our attention.

This course offers students a comprehensive understanding of cities and urban challenges from the perspective of sustainable development. It examines the historical, social, economic, cultural and environmental dimensions of cities. As the course focuses on urban issues at global, regional and local levels, students will not only gain knowledge about the causes and impacts of urban problems, but also learn how different stakeholders can help achieve sustainable liveable cities in the age of globalization. By adopting an issue-based approach, different SDGs such as no poverty (SDG 1), industry, innovation and infrastructure (SDG 9), reduced inequality (SDG 10) and sustainable cities and communities (SDG 11), will be included in a variety of topics ranging from socio-economic to environmental aspects in the course. Different cities will be chosen as case studies for understanding various urban challenges and solutions. Apart from lectures, students will develop critical thinking on various urban challenges that affect their daily lives through video discussion, group projects and presentation in the discussion forums.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
Not for Geography and Resource Management Majors.
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.course_attributes:
SDG-GE #1 No Poverty
SDG-GE #9 Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure
SDG-GE #10 Reducing Inequality
SDG-GE #11 Sustainable Cities and Communities
courses.learning_outcomes:
This course will offer students a comprehensive understanding of cities and urban challenges. As the course focuses on urban issues from global, regional and local perspectives, students will not only gain basic knowledge of the urban development process, but also learn how urban problems arise. This will be achieved by analysing the complex interaction between the physical environment and human society from the perspective of sustainable development. Parallel to the enrichment of knowledge, students should be able to improve their critical thinking and analytical capability through an issue-based approach in the teaching of this course.

After completing this course, students are expected to:

· be capable of understanding different urban challenges encountered by cities in the age of globalization

· be critical in interpreting sustainable/unsustainable development practices of different cities

· have increased exposure to different SDGs and solutions to urban challenges.
courses.course_syllabus:
By analysing the complex interaction between the physical environment and human society at different levels, students will learn and interpret different urban issues in relation to SDGs in different lectures. The issue of balancing economic growth and development of sustainable cities for all is the main theme that links various lectures.

Global perspective

· Urbanization and the evolution of cities

· Globalization: the emergence of world cities

· Networks of cities: a borderless world?

· Tour around major cities in the world: nature, functions and levels of development

Regional perspective

· The rise of economic regions in China: regional development in perspectives (SDG 9)

· The less developed countries: are cities growing too fast and too much? (SDG 10, 11)

· The more developed countries: are cities sustainable? (SDG 10, 11)

Local perspective

· Urban competitiveness of cities (SDG 9)

· Livability of cities: the urban quality of life (SDG 11)

· The urban landscape: from landmarks to city functions

· Informal settlements and poverty (SDG 1)

· Urban challenges: socio-economic and environmental (SDG 11)

· Planning for sustainable cities (SDG 11)

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Hong Kong Cinema
courses.description:
(UGEC2241 is double-coded with CURE2030.)

This course is a survey of Hong Kong cinema, with emphasis on its contemporary development. This course will cover both textual and contextual analyses: we treat Hong Kong cinema as an art form and introduce different ways to appreciate them and criticize them; we also read these films as commercial cultural products to investigate links between movies and society at-large. The course will introduce basic film theories and film languages. It helps students to reflect on their own relationship with these Hong Kong movies they grow up with, as well as help elucidate the complex relationship between Hong Kong cinema and Hong Kong identity.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGEC2241 is double-coded with CURE2030.
Not for Cultural Studies; Public Humanities, Religious Studies Majors; and students who have taken CURE2030 or UGEC2774.
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.learning_outcomes: Students are expected to acquire a general understanding of basic film languages, and to develop basic skill in film appreciation and film criticism. After the course students should be able to understand Hong Kong history and Hong Kong society from the perspective of cinema, with critical awareness of related issues pertaining to gender, class, and cultural identity. They are also expected to understand the intimate relationship between film production and film consumption, as well as the differences and connections between art and commerce in the domain of Hong Kong cinema.
courses.course_syllabus:
The lectures are organized historically, allowing students to gain a full picture of not only Hong Kong cinema’s history but also the history of Hong Kong. Students will also learn the basic techniques of film appreciation and film criticism through the various examples introduced. Lectures are composed of lecturer’s presentation and close analysis of selected film clips, through which students will be called attentions to specific stylistic, thematic, and theoretical issues. Combining the lectures and film analysis, students will learn both specific issues about Hong Kong cinema and acquire basic training of film analysis.

| **1.****Introduction: Basic Film Form** | |
| --- | --- |
| Required Reading: | Margo Kasdan et al.: *The Critical Eye: An Introduction to Looking at Movies* |
| | |
| **2.****Pre 1949 Hong Kong Cinema and Cantonese Films in the 1950s** | |
| Required Reading: | Yingchi Chu: *Hong Kong Cinema*, Ch. 1 |
| Optional Reading: | 譚春發:〈上海入港影人與香港電影〉(HKIFF 1994) |
| | 廖志強：《一個時代的光輝》29-49頁。 |
| | |
| **3.****New Generations in the 1960s** | |
| Required Reading: | 卓伯棠:〈六十年代青春派電影的特色〉(HKIFF 1996) |
| Optional Reading: | Poshek Fu: “Hong Kong and Singapore: A History of the Cathay Cinema” (Wong 2002) |
| | Edwin W. Chen: “Musical China, Classical Impression: A Preliminary Study of Shaws’ *Huangmei Dial Film*” (Wong 2003) |
| | |
| **4.****King Hu and Zhang Che** | |
| Required Reading: | David Bordwell: *Planet Hong Kong*, pp 248-60 |
| Optional Reading: | 胡金銓：《胡金銓武俠電影作法》頁96-125。 |
| | 陳默：《刀光俠影蒙太奇: 中國武俠電影論》頁337-356。 |
| | |
| **5.****Bruce Lee and Jacky Chan** | |
| Required Reading: | David Bordwell: *Planet Hong Kong*, pp 49-60 |
| Optional Reading: | Stephen Teo, *Hong Kong Cinema*, Chapters 8 & 9. |
| | Chiao Hsiung-ping, “Bruce Lee: His Evolution on the Kung Fu Genre” *Journal of Popular film and Television* 9.1 |
| | Shu Yuan, “Reading the Kung Fu Film in an American Context: From Bruce Lee to Jackie Chan” *Journal of Popular Film and Television* 31.2 |
| | |
| **6.****Hong Kong** **New Wave** | |
| Required Reading: | Law Kar, “An Overview of Hong Kong’s New Wave Cinema” (Yau 2001) |
| Optional Reading: | 卓伯棠:《香港新浪潮電影》。頁61-229。 |
| | |
| **7.****The Hui Brother and Other Commercial Films in the 1970s** | |
| Required Reading: | Jenny Lau, “Besides Fists and Blood: Michael Hui and Cantonese Comedy” (Fu & Desser, 2000) |
| Optional Reading: | 羅卡：〈十年磨劍許冠文〉(HKIFF, 1984) |
| | |
| **8.****Auteur in the 1980s & 1990s** | |
| Required Reading: | Rey Chow, “A Souvenir of Love” (Yau 2001) |
| Optional Reading: | Ackbar Abbas, “The New Hong Kong Cinema and the *Déjà Disparu*” (Abbas 1997) |
| | |
| **9.****Contemporary Commercial Cinema (I)** | |
| Required Reading: | Stephen Teo, *Hong Kong Cinema*, Ch. 16 |
| Optional Reading: | David Bordwell, *Planet Hong Kong*, pp 171-177 |
| | Emilie Yueh-yu Yeh and Darrell William Davis, “Warning! Category III: The Other Hong Kong Cinema” *Film Quarterly* 54.4 (Summer 2001): 12-26. |
| | |
| **10.****Contemporary Commercial Cinema (II)** | |
| Required Reading: | David Bordwell, *Planet Hong Kong, Ch. 8* |
| Optional Reading: | 羅卡:〈吳宇森與林嶺東比較〉(HKIFF, 1997) |
| | 何思穎:〈終極犯禁〉(張偉雄 2002) |
| | Tony Williams, “Space, Place, and Spectacle: The Crisis Cinema of John Woo” (Fu & Desser 2000) |
| | |
| **11.****Films about 1997: Death and Identity Crisis** | |
| Required Reading: | 石琪:〈八十年代香港電影的成就感和危機感〉(HKIFF, 1991) |
| Optional Reading: | Kwai-cheung Lo, “Transnationalization of the Local in Hong Kong Cinema” (Yau 2001) |
| | Natalia Chan Sui Hung, “Rewriting History: Hong Kong Nostalgia Cinema and Its Social Practice” (Fu & Desser 2000) |
| | |
| **12.****Hong Kong Cinema and Hollywood** | |
| Required Reading: | Laikwan Pang “Copying *Kill Bill*” *Social Text* 83 |
| Optional Reading: | David Williams: “Enter the Dragon: Interview with Ang Lee” *American Cinematographer* 82.1 |
| | |
| **13.****Films after 1997: Fruit Chan and Milkyway Image** | |
| Required Reading: | 彭麗君：〈銀河影象電影世界中的男性關係網〉《中外文學》 29.10 (March 2001) |
| Optional Reading: | 洛楓《盛世邊緣》第五章。 |
| | |
| **14.****Conclusion** | |
