courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Advertising and Society
courses.description:
(UGEC2638 is double-coded with COMM2840.)

In this course, students will analyze real life campaigns to understand major effects of advertising in everyday life. Students will examine topics like materialism and commercialization of friendship to see how advertising persuade consumers through subtle uses of messages. They will also decode the formation and shifting of values promoted by Government and green campaigns. The course will help students distinguish between authentic and mediated reality regarding topics on artificial perfection of body image, sexual objectification and gender stereotyping.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGEC2638 is double-coded with COMM2840. 
Not for GCOM; COMM Majors; and students who have taken COMM2840. 
Co-requisite: UGFH1000, UGFN1000 or UGFN1001 (only applicable to students admitted to the 4-year curriculum)
courses.learning_outcomes:
* *At the end of** **this course, students will:**

1. Identify the multifaceted effects of mass-mediated advertising messages along the dimensions of subliminal messaging, infusion of constructed meanings, and mediation of reality.

2. Recognize the impacts of advertising on personal and psychological development.

3. Critically analyze advertising messages and motivations.

4. Investigate beyond the seemingly superficial phenomenon created by advertising into deeper issues related to living attitude and social values.

5. Develop a curiosity towards real life campaigns that promote meaningful causes that benefit citizens in Hong Kong.
courses.course_syllabus:
* *I. SUBLIMINAL PERSUASION**

* *Week** **1****: Subliminal Messaging and** **Materialism**

This week will introduce briefly all three sections and emphasis more specifically on subliminal persuasion, using materialism, luxury branding in China as examples. China has replaced Japan as the second strongest economic power in the world in 2012. It has also surpassed the US to become the second largest luxury brand market in 2009, spending of $9.4 billion and accounting for 27.5% of total global sales of luxury brands in the world. The invasion of luxury brands will inevitably alter the spending culture in China. Luxury brand ads hint subtly that consumers using them belong to a higher social group in the society. Students will discuss the subliminal indicators of luxury brand ads and their effects on attitudes towards social status in the society.

* *Week** **2****: Commercialization of Friendship in Social Networking Sites**

Social networking sites are dominating the media landscape among young people. Many viral ads are circulated through the disguise of sincere recommendations from friends, who actually share information for an incentive. This class will look into the core of this issue: should friendship be commercialized for personal gains?

* *Week 3: Properties, Personal Loan Ads and Wealth**

The recent waves of glamorized Hong Kong properties ads disseminated in China led to grave consequences in raising the prices of flats in Hong Kong. The property ads In Hong Kong shape unrealistic desires in viewers to own a flat that demands a lifetime of hard earned salaries. Personal loan ads encourage a supposedly free life of spending with no responsibility. This lesson will discuss the dynamics of these subliminal messages in affecting one’s decision about how to spend his / her money.

* *Week** **4****: Political Campaigns and Elections**

The presence of political campaigns in commercial media becomes prevalent these days due to the rise of democratization in the election process. This lesson will look into the various tactics and subliminal strategies used and manipulated in political advertising and its effects on public participation in election.

* *II. MEDIATION OF REALITY**

* *Week** **5****: Beauty Ads and Body Image**

This lesson will start with an in-depth look at the concept of mediation of reality, using studies conducted in Hong Kong that relate beauty and slimming product / service ads to a negative self-perceived body image in both men and women. Teenagers, in particular, are finding their self in their growing years by comparing themselves to the models in the ads. The gap between the idealized self and actual self creates frustrations among many. To bridge the gap, some fall for crash diets and some for desperate acts of plastic surgeries.

* *Week** **6****: Stereotypical Sex Ads and Gender Roles**

This lesson looks into the relationship between commoditization and objectification of the body and the resultant stereotypical roles played by men and women. Students will be challenged to live beyond the fixed roles narrowly defined and presented in the pseudo-reality.

* *Week** **7****: Tutor King / Queen Ads and Education**

The educational system in Hong Kong requires students to acquire incessant training and upgrading. Many tutors chanced this opportunity to create fast track learning to success by advertising their success rate of getting a student to obtain an As. This form of promotion alters students’ mentality to learning. Tutors acting as opinion leaders in ads create a trend of commercialized education.

* *Week** **8**: **A “Copycat” Culture**

“Copycat” China refers not only to the many cases of copying of products but also the imitation of ideas and other intellectual properties in China. Advertising is an industry that is keen on creativity, the very core element that sustains the industry. Piracy will suffocate the creative industry in the long run. Students will scan the creative landscape in China and learn to identify the seemingly original creative reality.

* *III. INFUSION OF CONSTRUCTED MEANINGS**

* *Week** **9****: Freedom of Speech and the Press**

This class will start examining the idea about meaning and value driven advertising/ advocacy. Recent cases show that advertising is affecting the press in Hong Kong and the Mainland has been censoring a lot of ads online and outdoor. What is the value of commercial speech? How much would the commercial speech be compromised? Will the censorship erode creativity, global branding strategy and business development? Students will investigate the longstanding struggle of the protection of speech in Hong Kong and the mainland.

* *Week** **10****: Globalizing and Standardization**

Global brands launched by global advertising agencies bring about their unique culture from the country-of-origin into a foreign country. For a global brand to be successfully adopted by local citizens, the agencies need to accommodate the traditional culture. However, the global influence for standardization sometimes surpasses localization, resulting in a pervasive infusion of foreign values. The balance and consideration of both cultures are the center of discussion in this lesson. A few global campaigns around the world will be presented to demonstrate the delicate balance between globalization and localization.

* *Week 11: “Anti” Campaigns and Social Movements**

This lesson will look into the value-driven impact and of Public Service Announcements (PSAs), including the anti-smoking, anti-drug and anti-AIDS campaigns on society. The lesson will also focus on the development, planning and execution of those campaigns. It is the purpose of the lesson to raise the awareness of the students in these cause advertising campaigns.

* *Week 12: Earth Hour, Outdoor Light Pollution and other Green Campaigns**

Greenpeace and other environmental protection groups have been working on major campaigns to improve the living conditions of the world. A few successful green campaigns will be discussed in class to reveal their value shifting strategies.

* *Week 1****3****: Food, Drug Ads and Public Health**

World Health Organization has established the strong tie between advertising of high fat, salt and sugar (HFSS) food and obesity. While UK has exercised blanket ban of food advertising to children and many other countries following suit, the Broadcasting Authority in Hong Kong has only advised five cases in the past four years regarding HFSS food ads to children. This lesson will investigate the difficult path to policy change in the business sector in Hong Kong. Advertising of all medicinal products in Hong Kong is strictly controlled by the Undesirable Medical Advertisements Ordinance (Cap 231) (the UMAO). Section 3 of the UMAO provides for an outright prohibition against the publication of 'any advertisement likely to lead to the use of any medicine … for the purpose of treating … or preventing' any disease, disorder or abnormal physical state. However, some prominent direct-to-consumer prescription drug ads are produced and broadcasted in Hong Kong recently. DTC drug advertising will change the traditional beliefs towards doctor-patient relationship and the demand of name-brand drugs at the expense of generic or equally effective drugs. Students will explore the Campaigns of Gardasil and Cervarix to find out their use of Government bodies as façade to lure the public to consult doctors about the drugs.

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Indian Cultures and Society
courses.description:
(UGEC2655 is double-coded with ANTH2324.)
This course is an anthropological introduction to contemporary India, its social life and diverse cultures. India is home to more than a billion people, who look differently, speak various languages, practice different religions, and have distinct rituals, cuisines, and music. It is one of the world’s powers with global economic, political, and intellectual influence. Moreover, it shares important ties in the sphere of philosophy, religion, and medicine with China and other Asian societies. Therefore, students will learn about India’s place both in the world and the Asian region, including its ties with Hong Kong. Students will also develop an understanding of the country’s diversity and complex social phenomena such as kinship, communalism, caste system, tribe, third gender, and class. We will discuss pressing issues and theoretical debates related to globalization, sexuality, social movements, religious conflicts, public health, environment, and labor, and how these issues have been shaped by post-colonial politics and current transnational processes.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGEC2655 is double-coded with ANTH2324. 
Not for Anthropology Majors; and students who have taken ANTH2324 or 3324. 
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.learning_outcomes:
By the end of the course, students will be able to do the following:

* understand and analyze the diversity of Indian society in terms of region, caste, class, religion, language, gender, and other sociocultural divisions

* identify and compare the main ideas of various schools of philosophy and religion that originated in the Indian subcontinent

* describe and give examples of the everyday life of Indian women and men, as well as hijras, and other persons who identify differently

* explain the impact of India’s colonial history on its modern society

* examine the role of popular culture, political processes, and the global economy in the lives of Indians in India and abroad

* explain the cultural influences of India on the Asian region and the world

* critically assess the academic literature, news, and public discourses about various aspects of the Indian society
courses.course_syllabus:
* *Indian population in terms of linguistic, cultural, and regional diversity;**

Indo-European and Dravidian languages as the major linguistic groups in India; Differences between South and North India, special positions of the North East, Kashmir, and the Andaman islands

* *Caste system (*varna*, *jati*), Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes (indigenous groups)**

The division of the Indian society into castes and those considered to be outside the caste system. History and contemporary practices related to caste discrimination and privilege; the place of the indigenous groups known as tribals or *Adivasi*

* **hijra*** **(third gender)**

Communities of people who identify neither men nor women

* *India’s independence, Partition (of India and Pakistan)**

The significance of 1947 Indian independence from the British colonial rule, the establishment of India and Pakistan, and its impact on present-day India

* *Hinduism, Islam, Sikhism, Jainism, religious festivals (*Diwali*, *oli*)**

Different religions as well as their rituals, beliefs, festivals, political representations, and family practices

* *Arranged marriage, extended family, modern femininities and masculinities**

The significance of family relations and arranged marriages in India; different models of being a woman and being a man in India, specifically Indians imagine themselves as “traditional” or “modern” women and men

* *Yoga, Ayurveda, Indian sports and medicine**

History and contemporary practices of Indian health traditions of Yoga and Ayurveda; how Indians take care of their health and body, different sports they play

* *Curry and other Indian foods**

Regional differences in foods; the impact of colonial history on contemporary habits and preferences in eating, cooking and dining

* *Bollywood and Indian popular culture**

The cultural, economic, and political importance of the Bollywood industry; other regional film industries in India

* *Migration, Indians in Hong Kong**

Indian diasporas around the world; the history and current processes of migration of Indians to Hong Kong; the experiences of locally born Indians in Hong Kong

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Working Cultures – Understanding Work in the 21st Century
courses.description:
(UGEC2656 is double-coded with ANTH2550.)
Work is a central practice and system of human production, innovation, social organization, and cooperation. Focusing on the work-place as 'working cultures', this course offers an anthropological perspective to understand how work is a matter of place, time, power, and meanings. We will further explore how current social, technological, and cultural transformations of work can be understood across places, in their historic continuities and disruptions. Each week the course analyzes, discusses, and rethinks a key concept in the anthropology of work: Class, time, race, gender, value, craft, ownership, creativity, autonomy, discipline, etc. Through reading contemporary ethnographies of different workplaces and circumstances, students will learn the analysis and theory of work in familiar or unexpected forms, as well as in different organizational settings from the office, factory, workshop, home, to online platforms. This course will further give the students the tools to systematically connect their own observations of work to writing about working cultures.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGEC2656 is double-coded with ANTH2550. 
Not for Anthropology Majors; and students who have taken ANTH2550.
Co-requisite: Any course with "UGFH" or "UGFN" as prefix (only applicable to students admitted to the 4-year curriculum)
courses.learning_outcomes:
By the end of this course, students will be able to:

• Understand the intrinsic value of work as individual practice and social organization of human production.

• Understand work's cultural diversity as well as its more general structures and patterns.

• Know different anthropological perspectives of work and be able to use them in different contexts.

• Comprehend and be able to apply the different theories explaining transforming or stable relations,

organizations, and processes of work.

• Be able to use anthropological modes of understanding, to think critically and perceptively about one's society and the world.

• Read ethnography critically

• Understand how theory is used in doing ethnography and writing ethnography
courses.course_syllabus:
This course will be divided in two parts: ‘Part I: Issues of Work Reloaded’; ‘Part II: Think work Differently’.

Part I: Issues of Work Reloaded

This part will comprise about six weeks of instruction. After the introduction session, each week will explore a central social issue of work though a contemporary lens: Working Class I: Gender in the Global Factory, Working Class II: Race in Migration, Working Time, Working Place and Space, Working Self

Part II: Think Work Differently

This part comprises the final seven weeks and will aim to challenge traditional understandings of work by showing how the conditions, experiences, and organization of work have changed: Working Craft, Working on Demand, Working Service, Working for Free?, Working Skill, Working Platforms, Working Capitalism
courses.feedback_evaluation:
1. Course and Teaching Evaluation Survey
2. Internal Course Review
Others:
courses.required_readings:
* *Working Class I: Gender in the Global Factory**

Work-places are social institutions that reproduce various relationships of society. By analyzing international technology hardware-production in Mexico, we will see how different ways of task organization, hiring practices, and promotion regimes shape gendered production as much as class and gender relations beyond the factory.

Salzinger, Leslie. 2003. "Chapter 1: Ways of Seeing", "Chapter 4: Bringing Fantasies to Life: Panoptimex" + "Chapter 5: Re-formulating the 'Traditional Mexican Woman': Particimex". In: Genders in Production: Making Workers in Mexico's Global Factories. Berkeley: University of California Press.

* *Working Class II: Race in Migration**

Following high-skilled IT-workers from India to Berlin this ethnography explores how immaterial work in an office can be embodied and ascribed to race rather than skill. Understanding work and its hierarchies within transnational and local class aspirations and shifting racial stereotypes, we will explore the experience of work as both empowering and discriminatory.

Amrute, Sareeta. 2016. "Introduction: Cognitive Bodies, Cognitive Work (until p. 18)", "Chapter 2: The Postracial Body". In: Encoding Race, Encoding Class. Indian IT Workers in Berlin. Durham: Duke University Press.

* *Working Place and Space**

All work-places exist in multiples spaces from global markets, supply chains, nation-states, to cities. Through the case of women working the night shift in Mumbai's call-center industry, we will see how social mobility and the roles of women in relations of work, leisure, family, and self are impacted by social norms of who should work at night.

Patel, Reena. 2010. "Introduction (until p. 18)", "Chapter 4: Traveling at Night". In: Working the Night Shift: Women in India's Call Center Industry. Stanford: Stanford University Press.

* *Working Time**

Time is crucial for structuring work and pay. Contemporary work often reorganizes time and new, flexible time regimes revolutionize our understanding of the work day. This week explores, on the one hand, time as valuable or wasted in the contemporary NGO-industry. On the other hand, we will look at time in terms of flexibility, autonomy, but also as source of anxiety for independent taxi drivers in New York.

Sheldon, Zachary. 2021. "Managing the Humanitarian Workplace: Capitalist Social Time and Iraqi Refugees in the United States." In: Anthropology of Work Review 42 (1 ): 35- 46.

Occhiuto, Nicolas. 2017. "Investing in Independent Contract Work: The Significance of Schedule Control for Taxi Drivers". In: Work and Occupations 44(3): 268 - 295.

* *Working Self**

Work is often not just a job, but a part of ourselves. But where are the boundaries between work as what we do or who we are? Through the case of sex workers in San Francisco, we will discuss themes of authenticity in customer relations, alienation, self-fulfilment, control, and abuse in service work.

Bernstein, Elizabeth. 2007. "Chapter 4: The Privatization of Public Women". In: Temporarily Yours - Intimacy, Authenticity, and the Commerce of Sex. Chicago: University of Chicago Press.

* *Working Service**

Similar service work like taking care of people can take place in drastically different settings from someones house to a luxury hotel. While some workers perform their duties in public, others are confined to the domestic and intimate spaces of other people's living spaces. By contrasting the management of domestic helpers in Hong Kong with luxury hotel workers in San Francisco, we will think about how work discipline and evaluation influences performance, but also how the setting of work allows or prevents abuse.

Constable, Nicole. 2017. "Chapter 5: Household Rules and Relations." In: Maid to Order in Hong Kong: Stories of Migrant Workers. Ithaca: Cornell University Press.

Sherman, Rachel. 2007. "Chapter 2: Managing Autonomy." In: Class Acts- Service and Inequality in Luxury Hotels. Berkeley: University of California Press.

* *Working Craft**

Practices and skill of work are often passed on, refined, and perfected through generations of practitioners. The skill of artisans and craft-workers has often been romanticized as traditional and pure. Through the reinvention of bartenders as highly skilled and creative makers in postindustrial New York, this week aims to understand how professions transform, become 'cool', and reinvent themselves.

Ocejo, Richard. 2017. “Introduction: A Stroll through the Market” + “Chapter 1: The Cocktail Renaissance”. In: R. Ocejo. Masters of Craft: Old Jobs in the New Urban Economy. Princeton: Princeton University Press.

* *Working on Demand**

What is creativity? What is a copy? Exploring the world of the now famous, hand-painting makers of oil painting reproductions in Dafen village, we will see what makes an artist or a worker, as well as the shifting boundary between copyrights and copy.

Wong, Winnie. 2014. "Introduction: After the Copy (until p. 22)" + "Chapter 4: Step 18: 'Sign Vincent"'[selections]. In: Van Gogh on Demand - China and the Readymade. Chicago: University of Chicago Press.

* *Working Platforms**

Social media platforms drastically transform employment relationships, modes of contract and payment, but also meanings of profession and occupation. Through the online communities and marketplaces of game designers and car sharing drivers, we will see how work becomes more individualized or more embedded online.

Schwartz, David. 2018. “Embedded in the Crowd: Creative Freelancers, Crowdsourced Work, and Occupational Community”. In: Work and Occupations 45 (3). Pp. 255-282.

Surie, Aditi and Jyothi Koduganti. 2016. “The Emerging Nature of Work in Platform Economy: Companies in Bengaluru, India: The Case of Uber and Ola Cab Drivers”. In: International and Comparative Labour Studies 5(3): 1 – 30.

* *Working for Free?**

Why do we work for free? Is that even possible? By looking at seemingly 'un-worklike' activities of women and promoters in the international party circuits, we ask how we can understand work beyond traditional relations of employment or exchange.

Mears, Ashley. 2020. "Chapter 3: The Potlach" + "Chapter 4: Trafficking at Model Camp". In: Very Important People - Status and Beauty in the Global Party Circuit. Princeton: Princeton University Press.

* *Working Skill**

Who has skills? What are skills? Understanding the complex social organization of building football stadiums in Qatar, this week explores how people know what to do if it has never been done before. We will further understand skill as embodied practice and matter of recognition.

lskander, Natasha. 2021. "Chapter 3: How Skill is Embodied ... ". In: Does Skill Make Us Human? Migrant Workers in 21st Century Qatar and Beyond. Princeton: Princeton University Press.

* *Working Capitalism**

Is all work productive? Is all work meaningful? Through this critique of contemporary capitalism as unproductive system, we will discuss the meaning of work and how to imagine new work practices in the future.

Graeber, David. 2018. "Chapter 1: What is a Bullshit Job" + "Chapter 4: What is it like to have a Bullshit Job?". Bullshit Jobs -A Theory. New York: Anholt & Schuster. Selections.

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Interpersonal Violence
courses.description:
(UGEC2680 is double-coded with SOWK2200.)

The purposes of this course are to provide students a basic understanding of violence that takes place in our daily life in the contexts of intimate relationship, family, school and community and to arouse their consciousness of contemporary values of equality, human rights and social justice. Various concepts and theories of violence will be introduced to explore the nature, causes, consequences and trends of violence in different aspects of life. Emphasis will be placed on the issues of interpersonal violence in contemporary societies. Social policy and provisions to prevent and eliminate interpersonal violence will also be discussed.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGEC2680 is double-coded with SOWK2200. 
Not for Social Work Majors; and students who have taken SOWK2200.
Co-requisite: UGFH1000, UGFN1000 or UGFN1001 (only applicable to students admitted to the 4-year curriculum)
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon completion of the course, students will be able to:

1. Critically examine the contemporary perspectives, concepts and theories on interpersonal violence.

2. Analyze causes and consequences of interpersonal violence in different contexts.

3. Apply and integrate interdisciplinary theories of violence to evaluate current policy and strategies on tackling violence.

4. Reflect on ways to combat violence and help to promote a peaceful environment.

5. Appreciate the contemporary values of equality, human rights and social justice.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Common misunderstanding and myths of interpersonal violence.

2. Contemporary perspectives and theories of violence from anthropology, education, medical science, politics, psychology, sociology and social work.

3. Nature, causes, consequences and trends of interpersonal violence.

4. The needs of victims and perpetrators of interpersonal violence.

5. Risk and protective factors of violence at personal, family, school, community and policy levels.

6. Issues of violence in regard to different genders, ages, classes, sexual orientations and cultures.

7. Current social policy and prevention programs on interpersonal violence.

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Social Innovation and Social Change for Good
courses.description:
(UGEC2686 is double-coded with SOWK2204.)

This course prepares students to learn from the emerging practices of social innovations in tackling with challenges in social, economic, cultural and political development in Hong Kong, the Greater China region and beyond. Students will acquire analysis, knowledge and strategies of social innovations, the processes of social change in creating social values. The scope of social innovations includes that of social entrepreneurs or social innovators in NGOs, communities, social movements, non-profit organizations, and business as well as the public sector. The course will examine cases of social innovations that have catalyzed important positive social change through the market, the nonprofit sector, the community, the public sector as well as the spaces among those four sectors.

Experiential learning will be integrated along the course delivery, such as programmme visits, dialogue with social entrepreneurs, staff members or service users. Students are encouraged to experience these operations, so as to analyze the development issues, the social values and innovations in offering practical solutions to poverty and social inequalities, unemployment and livelihood, civic and community engagement, fair trade as well as sustainable development. The course will provide inspirations as well as know-hows for students who are concerned about the articulation of local with global and regional issues and like to develop innovative actions to change the society for good.

UGEC2686 is double-coded with SOWK2204.
Not for Social Work Majors; and students who have taken SOWK2204.

courses.course_syllabus:
v Development of Social Innovation in Hong Kong Regionally and Globally

o What is Social Innovation?

o Issues of social, economic, culture and political development and Social Innovations

o Development of Social Innovation in Hong Kong, Taiwan, Mainland China, South Korea, Europe and US

v Defining Social Innovations

o Contesting definitions in Social Innovations

o Defining features of Social Innovations: social justice, empowerment, social capital, community engagement, design thinking, collaboration, etc.

o Generic social innovation processes: prompts and inspirations, imagining solutions, creativity and innovation, participation, prototyping and pilots, resource mobilization and start-ups, sustainability and scaling up

o Deep scaling and transformative social innovation

v Social Economy: From Social Enterprise to Social Solidarity Economy

o Business model and social economy model

o From Corporate Social Responsibility to Social Enterprise

o From Social Solidarity Economy to Eco-village

v Social Innovations and the Environment

o Greening the Economy and Society

o Sustainable cities and human settlement

o Food security and small holder empowerment

v Social Innovations in the Public Sector

o How policy can maximize citizen participation

o Participatory budgeting and planning

o Policy environment for social innovations

v Site Visits to Social Innovations in HK

v Case Study of Social Entrepreneurship: Dialogue Experiences

v The social, economic, political and cultural impact of Social Innovations

o Defining and measuring Impact

o Deep scaling and scaling up impacts

o Transformative Social Innovations

v Pursuing Social Innovations for the Global Futures

courses.description:
(UGEC2693 is double-coded with SOWK2203.)

This course introduces to students the field of mission-driven social enterprise, which aims to achieve a balance between meaningful social returns as well as sustainable or competitive financial returns through their products, services and other business practices. Through examining the best practices in social entrepreneurship, it also stimulates students to explore how social enterprise can affect policy and facilitate system change. Experiential learning elements such as visiting social enterprises and having dialogue with social entrepreneurs, staff members or service users will be arranged in order to let students experience its operation, understand its practice as well as to enable students in realizing its possibilities in offering solutions to education, healthcare, environment, workforce development, international development and other societal issues. The course is valuable to students who may want to design or lead a mission-driven social enterprise, or want to contribute time and energy to help build the social sector through this emerging field.

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Cultural Perspectives of Chinese Medicine

meetings.instructor:
Dr. CHAN Sze Nga, 
Dr. LIN Wai Ling, 
Dr. LIONG Ching, 
Dr. MOK Tik Lun, 
Dr. TSOI Yan Wang

courses.description: The establishment and development of Chinese Medicine have been greatly inspired by traditional Chinese culture, including the ancient Chinese philosophy, religious doctrines and history. This course will provide the opportunity for students to possess a deeper understanding of the association between traditional Chinese culture and Chinese Medicine. The difference between Chinese and Western cultures will also be discussed.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
Not for students who have taken UGEA1881.
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.learning_outcomes:
完成本科後，期望學生能夠︰

- 提高對中華民族傳統文化的認知

- 描述中華文化（包括中國古代哲學、宗教、文學、歷史、民族、飲食）與中醫學及識別中醫學的關係

- 比較並指出中西醫學的異同

- 評價中華文化在中醫學中的地位

- 分析各種與中醫相關的文化現象與中醫學的連繫

(
courses.course_syllabus:
講授內容

（一）中醫的概念﹕中醫、中藥與中國文化

概括介紹中國醫學的起源、形成、發展、學術特點及與中國傳統文化的密切關係，討論嶺南中醫藥文化以及中西醫的比較。

（二）中醫藥與儒、釋、道家文化以及《易經》的關係

簡述中醫藥文化與儒、釋、道三家以及易經的關係：儒家的仁、禮、中庸之道與中醫藥環環相扣，相互參照；釋家「醫方明」與中醫理論相互吸收和影響；道家學說不少與中醫理論類通，同樣強調人與大自然的關係和天人相應；

《黃帝內經》對自然和人體的認識，及其辨證理論，一脈相承自《易經》。

（三）文學與中醫

以儒通醫。古代學者如陶潛、陸游、蘇軾、羅貫中、曹雪芹等都曾經行醫或有深厚的中醫造詣。 中醫不少著作是用詩、詞、歌、賦形式撰寫的，讀者容易記憶，文學著作亦有豐富的中醫內容。本節選讀唐詩、宋詞、紅樓夢、三國演義等文學著作，欣賞其中包含的中醫宇宙觀、生命觀、並討論其中所列舉的藥物，病案。

（四）中醫理論之應用﹕飲食、養生之中醫文化

從簡介舌診、臟腑辨證、君臣佐使組方原理，先讓學生認識中醫診治方法，再以飲食和養生兩方面闡述中華寶庫之一----中醫保健之道。食療：飲食養生，家居實用補藥，涼茶及保健湯水。養生：四季養生法，十二時辰養生法，休憩養生與七情養生。保健、養顏：按摩、穴位和藥膳。

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Sexuality and Culture
courses.description:
(UGEC2853 is double-coded with GDRS2002.)

This course aims at an interdisciplinary approach to the study of human sexuality and culture. Three main areas will be discussed: (1) ideas of sexuality: Chinese and Western conception of sexuality, modern theories of sexuality; (2) sexuality and ethics: ethical issues of sexual relationships; and (3) sexuality and society: gender problem; issues relating to homosexuality, pornography, and AIDS.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGEC2853 is double-coded with GDRS2002. 
Not for Philosophy Majors; and students who have taken GDRS2002. 
Co-requisite: UGFH1000, UGFN1000 or UGFN1001 (only applicable to students admitted to the 4-year curriculum)
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Identify and recognize the ideas of sex and sexuality in Western and Chinese culture.

2. Discuss sexual issues openly and intellectually without any taboo.

3. Identify and reflect on various media representations of sex.

4. Appreciate the complexity of sexuality in modern society regarding homosexuality, feminism, etc.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. 導論：性、性事與文化（Sex, Sexuality and Culture）

2. 性事的思想

2. 1西方傳統性事理論

2. 2中國傳統色情思想

2. 3現代性事思想革命及其發展

3. 性事的表象

3. 1裸體／性事藝術之演變

3. 2身體 ／性器官之文化意義

3. 3性事與文字：色情書

3. 4性事與電影：藝術與淫褻之間

4. 性事的制度

4. 1性、愛與婚姻

4. 2「同性愛」

4. 3性別、性別主義與女性主義

5. 結論: 性事之意義

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Animals and Society : A philosophical approach
courses.description:
This course examines the social issues of animal from the philosophical perspectives. What are the key differences humans and other animals? Is it ethical to raise and slaughter animals for food, conduct tests on, and buying or keeping animals as pets? How ought one to respond to unethical laws on animals? What are the social impacts of these actions? Students will develop an interdisciplinary perspective in the fields of animal studies, ethical theory, sociology and environmental philosophy by understanding how ancient and contemporary philosophers, sociologist, environmental scientists, ecologists, activists, and journalists have responded to key ethical questions concerning animal rights and its social implication. Topics to be discussed include: animal-machines, ethical meat eating, radical vegetarianism, and the evolution of pets.
This course bears on several of the United Nations’ Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). By transforming our attitude towards and relationship with animal, we enjoy the mental health benefits of a deepened appreciation of and connection with them (SDG#3 Good Health and Well-Being). By exploring the key difference between human and animals, we deepen our understand toward ourself and different species on earth (SDG#14 Life below water & SDG#15 Life on land). Finally, by reimagining our relationship with animal, we can envision for a just society in which all species could co-exist peacefully (SDG#16 Peace, Justice and Strong institutions).
courses.enrollment_requirement:
Not for Philosophy Major.
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.course_attributes:
SDG-GE #3 Good Health and Well-being
SDG-GE #14 Life Below Water
SDG-GE #15 Life On Land
courses.course_syllabus:
1. **Introduction:** **Animal in Our Society**

1. 1 What is “Animals”? the Differences between Human and Non-human

1. 2 The Role of Animal Domestication in Human Society: A Historical Overview

1. 3 The Current Situation of Animals in Our Society

1. **The Image of Animals** **in Our Society**

2. 1 The Western Tradition: Mythology, Literature and Social status

2. 2 The Chinese Tradition: Mythology, Literature and Social status

1. **The Moral Status of Animals**

3. 1 Animal Welfare Vs Animal Rights

3. 2 Utilitarian Approach

3. 3 Right-based Approach

3. 4 Capabilities Approach

3. 5 Ecofeminist, Contractarian and Virtue Ethics Approaches

Part 1-3 will focus on the problem of Human and animal broder from various academic discipline, student will have a better understanding toward human and different species on earth

(SDG#14 Life below water & SDG#15 Life on land).

1. **Animals as Food** : **Meat industry**
1. 1 A History of Meat Eating
2. 2 Modern Intensive Farm : A Case Study
3. 3 To Eat or Not to Eat
4. 4 Vegetarianism as Social Movements

1. **Animals as Tools:** **Medical and Entertainment Industry**
1. 1 Animal Experiment
2. 2 Zoos and Entertainment
3. 3 Animal as Working Tools

1. **Animals as Pet and Commodity:** **The social phenomenon**
1. 1 Is it Wrong to Buy and Sell Animals?
2. 2 Commodification of Pet Species
3. 3 Ethics of Keeping Pets
4. 4 *Pet-o-philia*
5. 5 The Death of pets

In part 6, we discuss the emotional and spiritual connection with pet animals, student's may thus reflect on their attitude towards them, and hopefully would enjoy the mental health benefits by a deepen appreciation of and connection with animals. (SDG#3 Good Health and Well-Being).

* *7. Conclusion:**

7. 1 Peaceful Co-existence: The Relation of Human and Animals

7. 2 Animals as Family Members, Companion and Social Members

7. 3 The Road to Citizenship in Our Society

In part 7, By exploring the theory of animal citizenship, students will be able to reflect on the possibility and feasibility of a just society in which all species could co-exist peacefully (SDG#16 Peace, Justice and Strong institutions).

courses.required_readings:

“History’s Biggest Fraud” in Yuval Noah Harari. *Sapiens: A Brief History of Humankind.* New York: HarperCollins, 2015, pp.77-98.

“Why Is It So Hard to Think Straight About Animals?” in Hal Herzog. *Some We Love, Some We Hate, Some We Eat.* USA: HarperCollins, 2010, pp.1-14.

* *Week 3-5:**

* *Week 8-9:**

“Pet-o-philia: Why do Humans (and Only Humans) Love Pets” in Hal Herzog*, Some We Love, Some We Hate, Some We Eat.* USA: HarperCollins, 2010, pp.67-96.

* *Week 13-14:**

“Improving Empathy” in Lori Gruen. *Entangled Empathy. An Alternative Ethic for Our Relatioships with Animals.* USA: Lantern Books, 2015, pp.81-26.

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Economic Reform and Social Impacts in China
courses.description:
(UGEC2883 is double-coded with SOCI2106.)

Focusing on economic reforms, students will be taught the impetus of the institutional changes in China and how they affect the cultural, socioeconomic and organizational lives of Chinese people. Key social science concepts and theories like institution, ideology, socialism, marketization, social control, civil society, industrialization, urbanization, stratification, family, socialization, and specialized terms in China studies like guanxi (關係), danwei (單位), hukou (戶口), harmonious society, China dream, floating population, rural-urban divide, one/two child(ren) policy, will be introduced and discussed from an integrated perspective. After taking this course, students should be able to grasp the transformation and development in key areas of China, including economic reform, political system and change, rural and urban development, social inequality, family and marriage, cultural and ideological issues, as well as other institutional developments.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGEC2883 is double-coded with SOCI2106.
Not for Sociology Majors; and students who have taken GESC2118, SOCI2106 or UGEA2190.
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.learning_outcomes:
• Students are expected to grasp the basic knowledge about China’s major social institutions such as socialist market economy, party-state apparatus, stratification system, and lineage values. They should also be able to understand the evolution of these institutions over the past several decades.

• Students should be able to understand how social scientists analyze and examine the formation, evolution, and influences of the major social institutions in contemporary China. They are expected to grasp the key concepts and theories developed from social scientists from sociology, economics, political science, etc.

• Besides basic facts and social science perspectives, students are encouraged to critically evaluate some controversial topics from different angles through writing assignments. The topics will include key dilemmas in contemporary China regarding economic growth and social equality, state capacity and democracy, traditional culture and modernization, etc.
courses.course_syllabus:
* *Week 1: Introduction: Basic Information of the Chinese Society**

\* Provide basic information about the socio-economic characteristics of the contemporary China.

* *Week 2 & 3: China before Reform Era**

\* Provide information about the political systems, economic institutions, and social control mechanisms of China during Mao’s era.

* *Week 4** **& 5: Economic Reform**

\* Provide information about the processes of economic reform since 1978, as well as its impact on the economic system afterwards.

* *Week 6 & 7 & 8:** **Impacts on the Political System and the Preservation of the Party-state**

\* Provide information about the impact of the economic reform on the political system in China afterwards. Also, we will discuss the puzzle regarding the preservation of the Party-state despite the massive political reform.

* *Week 9****: Impacts on Rural-urban Development and Migration**

\* Provide information about the differential impact of the economic reform on the urban and rural development, as well as the resultant great migration since then.

* *Week 10: Impacts on Social Stratification and Inequality**

\* Provide information about the impact of the economic reform on the transition of the social stratification system and the enlarging social inequality.

* *Week 11 & 12: Impacts on Social Conflicts, Civil Society, and Social Control**

\* Provide information about the impact of the economic reform on the growing social conflicts and the newly emerged social control mechanisms.

* *Week 13: Impacts on Population and Demographic Transition**

\* Provide information about the impact of the economic reform on the population and demographic patterns.

* *[\*whether covering this depending on our schedule, because we may save some time for students to finish the final paper before the due date\*]**

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Money, Land and Housing

meetings.location: Lee Shau Kee Archi Bldg G02
Lee Shau Kee Archi Bldg G02

courses.description: When money is fiat, land is scarce, housing becomes a good hedge of inflation. However, when housing is both an investment capital as well as a necessity, the city is split into an owner-renter dichotomy, with the former preferring an increase of housing prices but just the opposite for the latter. When the pendulum of housing price swings to one side, housing capital can result in negative equity, asset bubble, or even financial crisis. Swinging to the other end, housing can become unaffordable, even if public housing or subsidy can be provided. Decent living environment and sustainable community are the concerns of cities. This introduction course explores the reasons of the housing price fluctuations and the implications of the dichotomy on the city lives.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
Not for Urban Studies Majors. 
Co-requisite: UGFH1000, UGFN1000 or UGFN1001 (only applicable to students admitted to the 4-year curriculum)
courses.course_syllabus:
The following topics are included in the course:

PART I: Relationships between money, land and housing

1. Why housing can be a good hedge of inflation?
2. How does the monetary policy affect the housing market?
3. Why interest rate and inflation rate affect housing price?
4. Is high land price or restrictive land supply a cause of high housing price?
5. How does the housing policy affect the housing market?

PART II: Social implications of housing issues

1. What is the owner-renter dichotomy of housing?
2. What are the implications of the housing dichotomy on our city lives?
3. Why housing can become unaffordable?
4. How does housing price fluctuation affect financial stability and wealth inequality?
5. Why are there housing cycles and housing bubbles?
6. What is property hegemony?

PART III:

1. Is public housing or cooperative housing a solution?
2. A sustainable housing policy.

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Belt and Road for Collaborative Modernization
courses.description:
(UGEC2917 is double-coded with URSP2010.)

The Belt and Road initiative (BRI) refers to the Silk Road Economic Belt and the 21st Century Maritime Silk Road. It is a significant development strategy proposed by the Chinese Government spanning approximately 60 countries in Asia, Eastern Europe, Middle East and Africa, with a total population of 4.4 billion. This initiative takes advantage of global transportation routes, core cities, and key ports to build international economic co-operation corridors. The effective operation and management of the BRI require collaboration among a broad range of disciplines comprising civil engineering, material science, government operations, economics and finance, social and political science, and environmental science. A critical and multi-disciplinary understanding of the BRI can provide insights into basic requirements, geopolitical impact, physical barriers, and sustainability potential of the infrastructural projects it proposes to undertake and help relevant stakeholders address large-scale and complex challenges in the lifecycle management of those infrastructure projects. This course involves forward-looking perspectives to assess, predict, influence and enhance the in/direct network effects and benefits of the BRI on the cities, urban systems and peoples of the Belt and Road region.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGEC2917 is double-coded with URSP2010. 
Not for Urban Studies Majors; and students who have taken URSP2010.
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.course_syllabus:
Introduction

1. Globalization in Perspective: introduction of globalization as a term, process and ideology.

2. Historical Silk Road: historical origins of the Silk Road; continuities and shifts in terms of Maritime Silk Road, interactions and encounters along the Silk Road.

Global Implications

3. Geopolitical Shifts: branding and popularizing the Belt and Road Initiative; imperial histories and ambitions informing the initiative.

4. Eastern and Western Perspectives: the opportunities and challenges arising from Belt and Road Initiative with reference to the European Recovery Program; the geopolitical theories of Alfred Mahan, Halford Mackinder and Henry Kissinger; the philosophy of Confucius and Taoists.

Facilities Connectivity

5. Construction Management in BRI countries: risk management in international construction joint ventures and overseas development institute; large-scale transnational high-speed railway construction projects.

6. Green and Sustainable Urbanization: In search of better livability in BRI cities: Liveability in dense and vertical cities; green finance; carbon auditing and carbon trading; green strategies for achieving sustainable development goals.

Unimpeded Trade

7. Trading, Logistic, and Professional Service: Hong Kong as regional BRI centre for trading, logistic and professional services; manufacture shifting and upgrading experience in ASEAN economies.

8. The Energy Market: energy market opportunities and uncertainties in Northeast, Southeast, Central Asia and Africa; global oil and gas markets; renewable and sustainable energy goals.

Financial Integration

9. The Bond Market: infrastructure financing; international bond market and Hong Kong’s debt market; corporate social responsibility on Green Bond Issuance; the Asian Infrastructure Investment Bank and the Silk Road Fund.

10. International Settlement: currency wars and special drawing rights; consumerism and RMB internationalization; Hong Kong as the offshore RMB centre; the New Development Bank.

Regional Responses

11. Middle East: geopolitics and energy nexus; religious and national concerns.

12. Central and Eastern Europe: rail and maritime connectivities; integrating trade and resisting xenophobia.

13. India: regional rivalry, economic integration and border disputes

courses.description:
(UGEC2925 is double-coded with LING2301.)
This course explores human language in the broader context of culture and society. How, for example, does one language community interacts with another language community in the multilingual world? Does language change and what are the processes involved? To what extent does the same language vary due to gender, power, status, and identity? How do men and women, people with higher and lower social status talk differently? How are these relationships reflected in the use of politeness markers and other linguistic devices? What are the implications of sociolinguistics on education and language planning?

Through introducing students to major issues in sociolinguistic studies, students will be able to develop a better understanding of how language functions in societal contexts from a linguistic perspective. Discussions will also focus on common sociolinguistic phenomena in daily communication, with a specific emphasis on the use of various languages and dialects spoken in Hong Kong. Exploring these topics will enhance students’ awareness of language as a cultural phenomenon and sharpen their sensitivity to the nuances of language use in the context of a multilingual world, with special reference to the sociolinguistic context of the Greater China area.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGEC2925 is double-coded with LING2301.
Not for Bimodal Bilingual Studies; Linguistics Majors; and students who have taken LING2301 or UGEC1925. 
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix.
courses.course_syllabus:
Languages, dialects and varieties

* Language vs. dialect, language standardization, regional dialects, social dialects, styles, registers and genres

Multilingual societies and discourse

* Multilingualism, diglossia, code-switching, language shift, maintenance, and revitalization

Contact languages

* Lingua francas, pidgin and creole formation, linguistic characteristics of pidgins and creoles

Language variation and change

* Regional variation, linguistic variables, social variation, gender variation, language change

Language, gender and sexuality

* Sexist language, gender and sexuality identities

Sociolinguistics and education

* Social dialects and education, multilingual education, English as a global language

Language attitudes and identity

* Language attitudes and effects on identity; how identities are expressed through variations of language

Language policy and planning

* The goals of language policies, the role of government and language users, the roles of ethnic and national identities

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Islam in Hong Kong
courses.description: This course aims to address the historical development and localisation of Islam in the cultural context of Hong Kong. It firstly introduces the basic knowledge of Islam and Islamic culture worldwide; and the prominent cultural and social position of Islam in the contemporary globalised world. It then applies Hong Kong as a case study to examine the historical evolution of various Muslim communities of Hong Kong, such as Indian, Pakantsian, Indonesian, African and Chinese Muslim communities. It also discusses how these Muslim communities interfaced with each other and integrated with the local society. By establishing a more inclusive and diversified understanding of the local Muslim community, this course responds to the UN Sustainable Development Goals #11 'Sustainable Cities and Communities. Meanwhile, it pinpoints local Muslim communities' challenges in the current globalised world. Islam is one of the most important religions in the contemporary world, which is also a key to achieving a safe and peaceful global environment; therefore, this course also echoes the UN Sustainable Development Goals #16 'Reduced Inequalities'.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
Not for History Majors. 
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix (only applicable to students admitted to the 4-year curriculum)
courses.course_attributes:
SDG-GE #11 Sustainable Cities and Communities
SDG-GE #16 Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Students should understand core concepts and theories related to one of the most prominent religions in the contemporary world – Islam and Islamic cultures worldwide. They shall learn that understanding Islam is core to developing a safe and culturally inclusive world enourgaed by the UN Sustainable Development Goals #16 'Reduced Inequalities' and 'Peace, 'Justice and Strong Institution'.

2. Students should learn about the historical development of Islam and various Muslim communities in Hong Kong.

3. Students shall develop their capacity to review the social roles of Islam and Muslim cultures in Hong Kong society.

4. Students shall be able to outline the importance of respecting and appreciating cultural diversity in both local and global contexts. Understanding and respecting social diversity is also the key to making an inclusive and sustainable global community outlined by the UN Sustainable Development Goals #11 'Sustainable Cities and Communities.
courses.course_syllabus:
Section 1: Understanding Islam

1. The origin of Islam and its historical development.

2. Islam and its cultural diversity from a global perspective.

3. The cultural and social features of Islam and Muslim communities.

4. Islamic world in a global context.

Section 2: Islam in Hong Kong

1. The historical transformation of Islam in Hong Kong.

2. The introduction of various Muslim communities in Hong Kong.

3. The localisation of Muslim communities in Hong Kong.

4. The roles of local Muslim communities from pollical, economic and social aspects.

5. The social roles and contribution of local Muslim communities.

Section 3: The future development of Islam in Hong Kong

1. The worldwide challenges of inheriting religious traditions in the contemporary world.

2. The current challenges faced by the local Mulsim communities.

3. The sustainable incorporation and integration between Muslim communities and Hong Kong society.

第一部：認識伊斯蘭

1. 伊斯蘭的源起和歷史演變。

2. 環球視野下的伊斯蘭及其文化多元性。

3. 伊斯蘭文化和穆斯林社群的社會及文化特徵。

4. 全球化下的伊斯蘭世界。

第二部：伊斯蘭在香港

1. 伊斯蘭在香港的源起。

2. 簡介香港不同穆斯林社群的形成和發展。

3. 香港穆斯林社群的本地化歷程。

4. 香港穆斯林社群在政治、經濟及社會面向的發展概況。

5. 香港穆斯林社群與香港社會的聯結及貢獻。

第三部：展望本地伊斯蘭的宗教承傳及發展

1. 討論當代全球化世界中宗教傳承的變奏及其所面對的挑戰。

2. 本地穆斯林社群在宗教傳承上所面對的困難及機遇。

3. 本地社會與穆斯林社群如何連達可持續的協作和融合。
courses.feedback_evaluation:
1. Course and Teaching Evaluation Survey
2. Internal Course Review

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Museum and Heritage
courses.description: This course reviews the historical development of concepts and theories related to museums and cultural heritage. It introduces diversified definitions of museums and heritage in the contemporary. Through selected case studies and field trips, it demonstrates the operations of museums and heritage sites; and analyses their social roles in modern society. It aligns with the UN Sustainable Development Goals #11 'Sustainable Cities and Communities, which emphasises making more inclusive and sustainable cities and communities. It also reviews the historical evolution of international conventions related to museums and heritage outlines how global and regional cooperation, such as the development of UNESCO World Heritage, is vital for sustainable heritage conservation and management. It directly responds to the UN Sustainable Development Goals #17, 'Revitalise the Global Partnership for Sustainable Development which suggests that successful global development requires inclusive and international partnerships.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
Not for History Majors; and students who have taken HIST3441. 
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.course_attributes:
SDG-GE #11 Sustainable Cities and Communities
SDG-GE #17 Partnerships for the Goals
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Students should understand core concepts and theories related to museums and heritage in the contemporary world.
2. Students should learn about the historical evolution of social and cultural roles of museums and heritage, especially in the social context of the East Asian region. They shall further develop their capacity to review the social functions of museums and heritage in the rapidly changing contemporary society. It shall help students pinpoint the core values of the UN Sustainable Development Goals #11 'Sustainable Cities and Communities, which emphasises making more inclusive and sustainable cities and communities.
3. Students shall be able to outline the importance of international and regional cooperation on issues related to heritage conservation, such as the establishment of UNESCO and the *Modus Operandi* of the UNESCO World Heritage. It allows students to recognise the importance of global solidarity in achieving a successful and sustainable international development emphasised by the UN Sustainable Development Goals #17, 'Revitalise the Global Partnership for Sustainable Development.
courses.course_syllabus:
Section 1: Concepts and theories related to museums and heritage

1. The origin of the museum and its historical development.

2. The concepts and definitions of cultural heritage and their historical evolution.

3. Understanding and interpreting cultural heritage in the East Asian context.

Section 2: The social roles of museums and heritage and their public engagement

1. The transformation of the social roles of museums and heritage.

2. The educational functions of museums and heritage: how museums and heritage sites could collaborate to provide a culturally diversified learning experience for different society stakeholders, including children, the elderly, and people with physical and mental disabilities.

3. The social inclusion of museums and heritage: how museums and heritage could help motivate local societies' sustainable development and address the value of social justice worldwide.

4. The reuse and revitalisation of heritage in modern society.

Section 3: Achieving sustainable heritage conservation and management through International and regional cooperation

1. International conventions related to heritage conservation and management.

2. The *Modus Operandi* of UNESCO World heritage and its sustainable development goals.

3. The challenges and opportunities of international and regional cooperation regarding heritage conservation and management.

第一部：博物館及文化遺產的概念和理論

1. 博物館概念的源起、發展和擴充。

2. 文化遺產概念的源起及發展。

3. 東亞地區對於文化遺產概念的理解和演繹。

第二部：博物館及文化遺產的社會角色與公眾參與

1. 博物館及文化遺產社會角色的歷史演變。

2. 博物館和文化遺產的社會性教育功能：兩者如何共同協作為不同的社會群體例如兒童、長者和身心障礙人士提供文化多元和具包容性的學習體驗。

3. 博物館及文化遺產與不同社群公眾的關係和連結：兩者如何有助社區營造的過程以及確立社會公義的重要性。

4. 文化遺產在當代社會的活化和再利用。

第三部：國際及地區性合作與可持續的文化遺產管理

1. 有關文化遺產保育和管理的國際性公約和守則。

2. 聯合國世界文化遺產的運作及管理機制；以及其可持續發展目標。

3. 國際及地區性合作在文化遺產保育上所面對挑戰及機遇。

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: History of Hong Kong
courses.description: This course introduces the history of Hong Kong from a British colony to the Special Administrative Region of China. It covers major events and issues of Hong Kong, emphasizing the development of political system, social structure and economic pattern, and evaluate the role of Hong Kong in modern China, in the Asian-Pacific region and in the world. The course critically examines how colonial governance and Sino-British relations affected Hong Kong, offering students a multi-perspective in understanding how Hong Kong has becomes what it is today. This course is relevant to three Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), particularly in the area of SDG#8 Decent Work and Economic Growth by looking at the development of Hong Kong from an entrepôt to a global financial centre, SDG#10 Reduced Inequalities by looking at how Hong Kong evolved from a society of racial segregation to social inclusiveness, and SDG#16 Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions by looking at the public policy in promoting social reform and social justice.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
Not for History Majors; and students who have taken GESC2150, GLBS3406. HIST2500 or UGEC3542.
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.course_attributes:
SDG-GE #8 Decent Work and Economic Growth
SDG-GE #10 Reducing Inequality
SDG-GE #16 Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Have a better understanding on the history of Hong Kong.
2. Recognize and critically analyze how and why various contexts affecting the development of Hong Kong.
3. Identify and examine the economic roles and positions of Hong Kong in modern Chinese history and in a global context (SDG#8 Decent Work and Economic Growth).
4. Articulate fair evaluation on issues surrounding the daily life in Hong Kong (e.g. inequalities in economic and political power, housing problem, etc.) after understanding their origins, and able to reflect on how to promote social reform and justice based on the experience of the past (SDG#10 Reduced Inequalities and SDG#16 Peace, Justice and Social Institution).
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Introduction
2. Opium War and the Opening of Hong Kong

* British Trade in China Before 1830s
* The War and the Cession of Hong Kong

1. Post-war Hong Kong: Economic Takeoff

* Positioning Hong Kong in the Korean War and Cold War
* “Made in Hong Kong”

1. Unrest and Crisis: Hong Kong in the 1960s

* A Changing Society
* The 1967 Riots and After

1. The MacLehose Years

* Building a Welfare State? - Social Reforms in the MacLehose Years
* Combating Corruption: Establishment of ICAC

1. Sino-British Negotiation and the Joint Declaration

* Conflict and Cooperation: Sino-British Negotiation
* Hong Kong Citizen’s Attitude Towards the Joint Declaration

1. Towards 1997: Hong Kong in the Transitional Period

* Challenges in the Last Years of Colonial Rule
* 1997 and Beyond

1. Conclusion

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Living Feminisms: Creating a Nonviolent and Collaborative Future
courses.description:
(UGEC3204 is double-coded with GDRS3006.)

Living Feminisms is all about how to put feminism into practice. Specifically, it is concerned with living out feminist consciousness in every step of making fundamental and lasting social change. With this in mind, Living Feminisms is not an exposition of feminist theories per se but an exploration of how social change effort would look like when we take up feminist consciousness that transcends separateness and duality. It explores the root causes that lead to the domination system that has plagued humanity for centuries, examines the possibility of having a fundamental and lasting social transformation that supports life of all, and takes a closer look at a few selected alternative practices such as restorative justice and sharing economies that hold out hope in times of crises.

The course also equips students with practical knowledge of being a change agent, introducing some practices with feminist consciousness that transform limiting beliefs about social change, nourish resilience by taking care of social and political trauma, and cultivate need-based facilitation skills and leadership that help navigate conflicts in the process of making internal as well as external transformation. To put it in a nutshell, Living Feminisms is a course that cultivates change agents with feminist consciousness.

Living Feminisms deeply resonates with the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) adopted by all United Nations Member States in 2015 as a blueprint for building a sustainable future by 2030. The course directly touches on alternative practices working towards the SDGs 5 (Gender Equality), 8 (Decent Work and Economic Growth), 10 (Reduced Inequalities), 11 (Sustainable Cities and Communities), 16 (Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions) and 17 (Partnerships for the Goals).

SDG-GE #5 Gender Equality
SDG-GE #8 Decent Work and Economic Growth
SDG-GE #10 Reducing Inequality
SDG-GE #11 Sustainable Cities and Communities
SDG-GE #16 Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions
SDG-GE #17 Partnerships for the Goals
courses.learning_outcomes: Upon completion of the course, students will acquire both knowledge and skills of social change that are in line with feminist consciousness. Specifically, they will be able to explain the roots of the domination system and a series of factors contributing to its perpetuation, and able to make change by transforming internal and external resistance. Students will be empowered to take lead in change effort through collaboration with others and mastery of conflict transformation skills. Students will learn to appreciate making a sustainable future requires transformation in areas broader than the conventionally defined economic realm.
courses.course_syllabus:
* *Class 1: How We Got Where We Are and Where Are We Heading?**

·Riane Eisler and Douglas P. Fry’s biocultural approach that explains the perpetuation of domination systems through the interaction between genes, brain development and culture, and points to ways where hope lies

* *Class 2: How Change Happens? A Lesson from Feminists**

·Investigating over 100 organizations in 15-year longitudinal research, Aruna Rao et al. develop a model of change that sheds lights on the mechanism of social transformation

·Cristina Bicchieri’s work delves into the formation and change of norms

* *Class 3: What Makes Things Stuck? Inaction Explained**

·How can we explain inaction of people witnessing immense suffering of others? Is the notion of false consciousness adequate? Joanna Macy, a Buddhist scholar and a veteran environmentalist, offers us some glimpse

* *Class 4: *Practice*: Retelling a New Story and Reconnecting to Life that We All Share**

·Understanding the impact of separateness at cognitive level is important but far from complete, Sharif Abdullah and Gretchen Ki Steidle offer us some practical advice on healing the deep-seated separateness by retelling a new story about human beings that fosters interdependence

* *Class 5: Taking Action: Violent or Nonviolent or Both?**

·Taking action to initiate change at various levels of a society is commonplace for activists. What is important is the kinds of action we undertake What is nonviolence? How does it work? What are its limits?

* *Class 6: Social Trauma and Social Transformation**

·Trauma is an overwhelming experience that outweighs the containing capacity of our nervous system How does trauma relate to and shape our political behaviors? Rather individualizing trauma as a single person experiences, how can we theorize collective trauma?

* *Class 7: *Practice*: Self-Care & Inner Resources**

·Change agents are prone to feel despair, powerlessness and hopelessness because they are more acutely aware of the enormity of suffering How to nourish our heart to make it open to pain and suffering while keeping the passion alive?

* *Class 8: Reconciliation and Justice: Exploring Transitional Justice**

·Common collective responses to atrocities are either forgetting or vengeance. Is transitional justice a viable third way? How does it work? What are the experiences of the 40 Truth and Reconciliation Commissions that have ever set up in human history can tell us?

* *Class 9: Sexual Violence: Exploring Restorative Justice**

·Our current justice system is a kind of retribution that punishes those who violate the law. How does it look like if we shift from retribution to restoration that focuses on needs and rebuilding of broken relationships? How controversial is it when applying to sexual violence?

* *Class 10: Collaboration and Feminist Leadership: Coming to Terms with Power**

·Activism often entails working with others. Is power necessarily evil? How does feminist leadership look like? How can we consciously use power in a way that does not subjugate but empower others? How can we replace power-over with power-with, power-to and power-within? Power-cube analysis

* *Class 11: Alternative Organizing - Holacracy, Sociocracy and Deep Democracy**

·There are always implicit agreements as to how to make decisions, define the nature and the scope of a task, evaluate outcome, and handle conflicts if we do not consciously decide how and what to decide

·Quite a many grassroots organizations have been experimenting with new ways of organizing that is more efficient than representative democracy in meeting the needs of all. These alternative forms of governance include Holacracy, Sociocracy and Deep Democracy.

* *Class 12: *Practice*: Workshop on Convergent Facilitation**

·Convergent facilitation, developed by the feminist Miki Kashtan, a form of facilitation that caters for both willingness and efficiency in order to come up an agreement, decision and/or concrete strategies that maximally work for the needs of all within a group. The workshop will cover the whole process from the start to the end convergent facilitation is a proven one as it was applied to a group who disagreed with each other about legalization of abortion

* *Class 13: Beyond Neoliberalism? Sharing Economy**

·Our current economies are profit-driven that more often than not serves a few but exploits most of other people Is it really hopeless to revise capitalism? Is Sharing Economy a viable alternative?

* *Class 14: Conflict Transformation: From the Personal to the International**

Disagreements and conflicts among activists are commonplace. However, acquiring ways of resolving and transforming conflicts within groups is a skill that is urgently needed but seldom given enough attention this last lecture will touch on conflict resolution and mediation.

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Media Representations of Minorities in Hong Kong
courses.description:
(UGEC3215 is double-coded with GDRS3214.)

How do we understand the notion of “minority”? And how does “minority”, look like, in cinema?

This course examines the rich tradition of critical film theory and provides students with an understanding of its main concerns and debates, concerning “minority” identities and subalternity. Addressing key discussions about media representations of “minority”, the course will look at the politics of representation and its impacts, both on- and off-screen. We will be looking at how media works to construct “the Other” in different contexts, and delve into important work which have inspired minority cinema, subaltern cinema, avant-garde cinema, local cinema, and independent cinema among other counter-movements, as well as recent re-conceptualizations and exploration of representations marginalized identities, positions, and experiences, local and abroad.

This course aims to introduces students to cinematic representations of differences as a productive paradigm of identity politics. While cultural theorist Stuart Hall suggests that identity politics is primarily a representation politics enacted through ever-changing conjunctures of differences, black feminist scholars bell hooks remind us that the margin is an open-ended position to create inside-out and outside-in connections. To examine the representation of minorities is therefore to cultivate an ability to embrace the margins, which allows us to ponder how the construction of “we” can be opened to “they,” the differences. With its divergent forms such as video art, film activism, commercial cinema and film system as well as film festivals, cinema provides a powerful medium for such a task. The question asked throughout the course would be: what are the normative representations of minorities in different forms of cinema? At the production level, to what extent could “minority” artists and theorists rebel against such normative representations—with what alternative visual images and narrative tools? At the level of spectatorship and reception, what kind of reading strategy could generate alternative politics of the minorities? What happened when digital platforms intervene into such endeavors of the minorities?

In the course, we will look at how socially marginalized groups, such as gay and lesbians, transgender persons, ethnic minorities, women in different stages of their lives, people with mental illness, teenagers, the elderly and many others are portrayed, and seek to expand their on-screen existence in Hong Kong media. In the exploration, we will engage with key questions in critical film studies, including spectatorship, authorship, popular culture, character stereotypes in genre films, self-representation, systems of media production, pro-sumer culture, (g)local cinema, alternative cinema, and new media. Students would be introduced to trailblazing works by “minority” filmmakers and media creators worldwide, and important cinematic and media movements that challenge the status-quo. Special focus will be placed onto important works in Hong Kong and Asia for critical examination and comparative studies.

Through critical exercise and reflection on notions such as power, diversity, equality, and freedom, the course aligns with the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) adopted by all United Nations Member States in 2015 by engaging directly with the SDGs 5 (Gender Equality) and 10 (Reduced Inequalities).

courses.course_attributes:
SDG-GE #5 Gender Equality
SDG-GE #10 Reducing Inequality
courses.learning_outcomes:
* Understand key theoretical concepts in film and media studies, as well as different modes of cinematic resistance to traditional social hierarchies
* Be equipped with the critical film and media research methods
* Relate the knowledge in critical film and media studies in their daily viewing and creative practices
* Develop their own critical judgement on popular film and media after considering relevant theoretical views
* Critically reflect on the relationship between film, media and society that produces, normalizes, and/or challenges gender and sexual inequalities
* Build capacity to understand and appreciate works by minority filmmakers
courses.course_syllabus:
1. What is Representation?: A View of the Margins (SDG 5 & 10)

- Setting up the tone of the course by introducing theories and concepts in relation to representations and margins

2. Hong Kong and the Minor (SDG 5 & 10)

- Introducing the idea of minor literature as a state of becoming to contest existing cultural identities and critical theories of Hong Kong

* *Part 1: Forms of Cinema as Media**

3. Engendering Hong Kong Cinema (SDG 5 & 10)

- Brief introduction of the history of Hong Kong cinema with a focus on the gendered and sexual meaning of its overt commercialism

4. A Feminist Reading of Hong Kong’s Independent Cinema (SDG 5 & 10)

- Investigating the ample representations and self-representations of gender and sexual minorities in the independent realm

5. Film Festivals: A Site of Social Actions (SDG 5 & 10)

- Explaining the significance of film festivals in enacting social changes for the minorities

- Introducing the way feminist and queer communities utilise film festivals to achieve their activist goal

* *Part 2: Figures of Minorities**

6. Sexualizing Hong Kong’s In-Betweenness (SDG 5 & 10)

- Examining how representations of sexual minorities construct Hong Kong cinema as a border that both separates Hong Kong from and connect it to East Asia

7. Coming-of-Age in a Postmetropolis (SDG 5 & 10)

- Reckoning with youth as both a status of identity and a representation that ascribes sexual and gender politics in a capitalist city

8. Aging Differently (SDG 5 & 10)

- Studying how the representation of aging serves as an analytical tool by which social inequalities in and the cultural identity of Hong Kong can be interrogated

9. Trans Cinema: Shimmering and Transing (SDG 5 & 10)

- Equipping students with conceptual tools such as aesthetic of shimmering and politics of trans-ing to critically engage with the debate between trans as an identity and non-identity politics

10. Ethnic Minorities: Constructing South Asian Men and Southeast Asian Women (SDG 5 & 10)

- Reflecting on the gendered and sexualized stereotypes of ethnic minorities and the very notion of ethnicity/race in Hong Kong

11. Crippling Cinema: Disability, Gender, and Sexuality (SDG 5 & 10)

- Learning from the representations of the disabled that how able-bodiness underpins our perception of sexualities

12. Are They Also One of Us? New Immigrants, Indigenous People, and Boat People (SDG 10)

- Questioning the boundary of Hong Kong identity by learning the history of the minorities excluded from the mainstream, popular, and official discourse

13. Queer Infrastructure: Mediating Life Within and Beyond Identity Politics (SDG 5 & 10)

- Introducing the queer conception of infrastructure as a living mediation of lifeworlds to prob with the possibilities of envisioning an open-ended Hong Kong’s future

courses.subject: UGEC
courses.title: Family and Society
courses.description:
(UGEC3224 is double-coded with GDRS3009.)

This course examines issues revolving the family as a social institution in different cultures. It explores processes of how the family is constructed in different forms as it intersects with various social systems including kinship, marriage, class, ethnicity, religion, and political economy. Particular attention is paid to how family as a gendered construction, while being a basic principle of social life globally, exhibits great diversity as it shapes and is shaped by local histories, traditions, and state policies. In an era of globalization and transnational mobility, the ideology and practice of the family are rapidly changing, but there are simultaneously trends of reversion and localization. Students will read ethnographies and develop cross-cultural perspectives on classic and contemporary issues concerning the family, such as work and domesticity, motherhoods and fatherhoods, sexual orientations, reproductive technologies, surrogacy and adoption. Students learn to critically think and articulate about family issues, and will acquire enhanced knowledge and skills in reflexive thinking and positive communication, cultural sensitivity, and diversity and inclusion.

The course is relevant to the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) adopted by all United Nations Member States in 2015, in particular SDGs 5 (Gender Equality), 10 (Reduced Inequalities) and 16 (Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions).

UGEC3224 is double-coded with GDRS3009.
